modules.module_verdict: The design module has the right architecture — an explicit, enumerable, seeded assignment policy with persisted balance and interference diagnostics — but several reproduced correctness bugs undermine it in production: stratified allocation crashes on feasible spaces, matched-pairs designs silently treat 50% of the universe past configured caps, supergeo grouping leaks across declared partitions, and the declared randomization space diverges from the actual mechanism (control subsampling, rerandomization filtering, registry blocks) in exactly the way that invalidates the package's assignment-aware randomization p-values. The most consequential methodology flaw is that candidate MDEs ignore test duration and shrink with accumulated pre-history, so the primary planning number misranks candidates on both axes.
strengths: AssignmentPolicy as an explicit, serializable randomization mechanism shared by design and inference is the right architecture; the fixed-count sampler was verified empirically to be marginally uniform over the declared space (20k draws, 6 assignments, counts 3284-3395), and seeds are handled deterministically via a policy-content digest for replay.

AssignmentMatrix's interval representation with treatment-overlap, treatment-control-conflict, and shared-control-usage validation (assignments.py + control_sharing.py) is clean, well-tested portfolio hygiene that correctly enforces 'shared controls yes, overlapping treatments no'.
Matched-pair features are built strictly from data before earliest_start (matching_window persisted in the candidate artifact), avoiding post-period leakage into design-time matching.

The analysis side honestly refuses to reconstruct stratified/matched_pairs/supergeo randomization spaces from a completed spec without market-level assignment metadata rather than silently pretending (orchestration.py:1173) — the right instinct, even though the fixed-count path still has the space-mismatch issue reported above.
Balance diagnostics are persisted separately from the objective score, with warnings rather than hidden hard gates, matching the documented design intent.

Candidate artifacts persist the full method-readiness bundle (policy execution metadata, interference diagnostics, calibration and inference configs), which is unusually good provenance for design decisions.

methodology_issues.current_approach: MDE = ncp * sd(pre-period daily treatment-control difference) / sqrt(n_pre_days) / baseline, computed on the full pre-history before the candidate start; the candidate's test duration is never an input.
methodology_issues.issue: The number that drives candidate ranking and roadmap optimization answers the wrong question. Power of a geo test depends on the post-period length (variance of the mean daily contrast over `duration` days, plus pre-period adjustment uncertainty), not on how many pre-period days happen to exist. Reproduced: the identical design shows MDE 0.0042 with a 2025-03-01 start and 0.0029 with a 2025-06-01 start purely because more pre-history accumulated, and 14-day vs 56-day candidates get identical MDEs. Candidates with later starts are systematically favored and longer durations get zero power credit — backwards on both axes.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Compute MDE for the actual test window: SE of the duration-day mean contrast (sd_daily_diff * sqrt(1/duration + 1/n_pre) style, or better, simulation/placebo-based power over historical windows of length `duration` as in GeoX/Trimmed Match practice). Pass duration and the planned estimator into the power calculation; the PowerSpec.placebo_windows field already exists but is unused here.

methodology_issues.area: Rerandomization and design-time filtering vs randomization inference (candidates.py _passes_rerandomization; inference has no counterpart)
methodology_issues.current_approach: Candidates are filtered by balance thresholds (SMD, variance ratio, trend difference), registry blocks, and interference-based control exclusion at design time, but the persisted AssignmentPolicy carries only the unrestricted space; grep shows no inference code references rerandomization.
methodology_issues.issue: Per Morgan & Rubin (2012), inference after rerandomization must be over the accepted assignment set; using the unrestricted space gives miscalibrated (typically conservative, power-wasting) randomization p-values and misstates n_feasible_assignments. The docs explicitly claim 'the same policy object is intended to drive design, randomization inference, and replay', but the executed mechanism (acceptance filter + control subsampling + blocks) is not encoded in the policy object.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Make the acceptance rule part of AssignmentPolicy (a predicate serialized with thresholds and the balance feature definition) and have enumerate/sample apply it, so design and inference share the true restricted space. At minimum, persist the acceptance rate and warn when randomization inference runs on the unrestricted space.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Greedy nearest-neighbor pairing anchored on the alphabetically-first available market, RMSE distance in z-scored feature space; features include mean, std, total, and trend per metric (mean and total are nearly collinear, double-weighting level).
methodology_issues.issue: Greedy alphabetical-anchor matching is well below the state of the art (optimal non-bipartite matching, Greevy et al.; Trimmed Match pairs on outcome comparability), can leave badly matched late pairs, and the arbitrary anchor makes pair quality depend on geo naming. Collinear features (mean vs total when histories are equal-length) silently re-weight the distance metric. n_pairs also caps pairs by group iteration order rather than globally best distances.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Use optimal non-bipartite matching (scipy-based blossom or distance-sorted global greedy at minimum), drop redundant features or use Mahalanobis distance, and select the n_pairs globally smallest distances. Report pair-distance distribution as a matching-quality diagnostic on the candidate.

methodology_issues.current_approach: SMD, variance ratio, and linear trend difference computed on geo-level mean daily values of only the first configured metric; fixed warning thresholds (|SMD|>0.25, VR outside [0.5,2]).
methodology_issues.issue: Balance is only checked on one metric even when the spec has multiple primary/guardrail metrics, and level/trend balance is a weak proxy for what matters to the default estimator suite (SCM/SDID care about pre-period fit and donor-pool predictive quality, not just level SMD). Two-observation geos get a polyfit 'trend' with no noise accounting; trend-difference thresholds are in raw metric units per day, so rerandomization thresholds are scale-dependent and not portable across metrics.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Compute diagnostics for all primary metrics, normalize trend difference (e.g. relative to baseline or as correlation of aggregate pre-series), and add a pre-period fit diagnostic (e.g. RMSPE of control-mean or synthetic-control fit to treatment aggregate) since the default suite is dominated by SCM-family estimators.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Scores balance over enumerate(max_assignments=200), i.e., the first 200 assignments in lexicographic combination order, and reports the best.
methodology_issues.issue: The first 200 lexicographic combinations concentrate on the alphabetically-first markets, so 'best_assignment' is chosen from a highly biased sliver of the space; for C(30,5)=142506 assignments, markets late in the alphabet are never considered for treatment.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Sample assignments uniformly via self.sample() when the space exceeds the cap, mirroring the enumerate-or-sample logic used elsewhere.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Metadata-inferred graphs create complete within-state (weight 1.0) and within-region (weight 0.5) cliques with duplicate edges for pairs sharing both; contamination_score sums weights over duplicates; spillover_sensitivity applies adjusted = observed + mean_exposure * spillover on a unitless grid.
methodology_issues.issue: Duplicate edges double-count in weighted_edge_sum and edge_count; the 1.0/0.5 weights are arbitrary and undocumented; the linear exposure-times-effect adjustment mixes unitless graph weights with outcome-scale effects, so 'adjusted_effect' has no calibrated interpretation and could be mistaken for a corrected estimate. InterferenceSpec.mode is also effectively ignored — any provided path wins regardless of declared mode.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Deduplicate edges (keep max weight), document weight semantics, and present spillover output strictly as a labeled sensitivity band (or drop adjusted_effect, keeping contamination flags and buffer exclusion, which are the useful parts). Validate that spec.mode matches the supplied artifact.

methodology_issues.area: Matched-pairs control pool (candidates.py, allow_unpaired_markets default True)
methodology_issues.current_approach: With allow_unpaired_markets=True (default), the policy universe includes unpaired markets which always land in control; randomization only flips pair orientations.
methodology_issues.issue: The realized design is a hybrid: matched-pair randomization but a control group diluted with never-randomized unpaired markets. Pair-based estimators (TBR on pair aggregates, paired iROAS) and the pair-flip randomization distribution do not describe the analysis contrast if estimators use the full control pool.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Default allow_unpaired_markets=False for matched_pairs, or clearly tag unpaired markets as 'donor-only' units excluded from the randomization contrast and from pair-based estimators.

drop_candidates.reason: Unitless graph-weight-times-effect linear adjustment produces an 'adjusted_effect' with no calibrated interpretation; contamination flags and buffer-based control exclusion already deliver the actionable part of interference handling. Keep the diagnostics, drop the pseudo-corrected estimate.

Duplicates candidates.py balance diagnostics with a biased lexicographic-200 enumeration, is used by no production code path (only one contract test), and its per-assignment SMD table overlaps rerandomization logic that lives elsewhere.

Behaves identically to fixed_treatment_count in every code path (enumerate, sample, inference reconstruction); it adds a spec value with no distinct semantics and invites the belief that candidate-level constraints are encoded in the randomization space when they are not.

bugs.summary: _stratum_treatment_counts allocates per-stratum treatment counts by raw stratum size, ignoring forbidden/fixed-control eligibility, so feasible stratified spaces are reported as empty and sampling crashes.
bugs.failure_scenario: markets A1-A4 (stratum A) and B1-B4 (stratum B), treatment_count=4, forbidden=(B1,B2,B3): allocation demands 2 treated in B but only B4 is eligible. n_feasible_assignments returns 0, enumerate() returns [], and sample() raises ValueError('Cannot take a larger sample than population') even though assignments like {A1,A2,A3,B4} are feasible. End-to-end, CandidateGenerator.generate_for_test crashes with an opaque ValueError('high <= 0') from rng.integers(0, 0) in _draw_policy_assignment (candidates.py:407), so stratified roadmaps with concentrated forbidden markets cannot plan at all.
bugs.evidence: Repro: n_feasible: 0; enumerate: []; sample raised: ValueError Cannot take a larger sample than population when replace is False. End-to-end: generate_for_test raised: ValueError high <= 0. Code: count = int(round(self.treatment_count * len(markets) / total)); count = min(max(count, 0), len(markets), remaining) — len(markets) is stratum size, not eligible size.

verification.explanation:
Reproduced both the unit-level and end-to-end failures exactly as claimed.

Unit level (src/fieldtrial/design/policies.py, _stratum_treatment_counts at lines 330-357): the allocator computes per-stratum treatment counts from raw stratum sizes — `count = int(round(self.treatment_count * len(markets) / total)); count = min(max(count, 0), len(markets), remaining)` — and the leftover-redistribution loop likewise uses `capacity = len(markets) - counts[stratum]`. Neither considers forbidden_treatment_markets or fixed_control_markets. With markets A1-A4/B1-B4 stratified by first letter, treatment_count=4, forbidden=(B1,B2,B3), the allocator demands 2 treated in stratum B where only B4 is eligible. Verified by execution: n_feasible_assignments returns 0, enumerate() returns [], sample(3) raises ValueError("Cannot take a larger sample than population when replace is False") from rng.choice in _sample_stratified, while p._is_feasible_treatment(("A1","A2","A3","B4")) returns True — so the space is genuinely non-empty by the policy's own feasibility predicate, and the policy passes __post_init__ validation (treatment_count <= total eligible).

End-to-end: built a 24-market synthetic panel (regions Midwest=5, Northeast=3, South=10, West=6), stratified policy with treatment_count=4 and 9 of 10 South markets forbidden. CandidateGenerator.generate_for_test crashed with ValueError("high <= 0") at src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py:407 (rng.integers(0, len(assignments)) with an empty enumerate() result reached via _draw_policy_assignment because n_feasible_assignments=0 <= treatment_count*20). There is no error handling in the generate_for_test loop, so the exception propagates and planning fails entirely with an opaque numpy error. A feasible plan (3 treated outside South + the 1 eligible South market) exists but is unreachable.

The scenario is realistic: forbidden/fixed-control markets concentrated in one stratum (e.g., a region already committed to another test or a permanent holdout) is exactly the use case these constraint fields exist for, and the existing integration tests only exercise stratified policies with no forbidden markets, so nothing masks it. Severity "major" is right: it is a hard crash of planning for valid configurations plus a silently wrong n_feasible_assignments=0 report, but not silent data corruption in successful runs.

bugs.summary: matched_pairs policies with no explicit treatment_count pair the entire market universe (n_pairs=None), silently treating ~50% of markets and ignoring the configured min/max_treatment_share caps.
bugs.failure_scenario: AssignmentPolicySpec(kind='matched_pairs') with treatment_count=None on a 30-market universe: construct_matched_pairs is called with n_pairs=None, builds 15 pairs, and treatment_count is set to len(pairs)=15. Every candidate treats 15/30 = 50% of markets while RoadmapDefaults.max_treatment_share = 0.20; a production geo test would put half the country into treatment, with the associated spend and holdout-loss consequences, without any warning.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: 'treatment markets: 15 share: 0.50 (default max_treatment_share=0.20)'. Code: pairs = construct_matched_pairs(..., n_pairs=policy_spec.treatment_count, ...); treatment_count = len(pairs) — min_t/max_t computed from treatment shares at lines 116-119 are never applied on the matched_pairs branch.

verification.explanation: The claim is accurate and reproduced. In src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py, the matched_pairs branch of _policy_context (lines 310-324) passes n_pairs=policy_spec.treatment_count directly to construct_matched_pairs; when treatment_count is None, the greedy pairing loop in matching.py (line 145: `while len(available) >= 2 and (n_pairs is None or ...)`) pairs the entire eligible universe, and treatment_count is then set to len(pairs). The share-derived caps min_t/max_t computed at lines 116-119 only reach the policy through default_treatment_count, which is used on the fixed/stratified/candidate_constrained branch (line 326) and the supergeo branch (line 281) but never on the matched_pairs branch when treatment_count is None. Reproduced on a 30-market synthetic panel with RoadmapDefaults (max_treatment_share=0.20): {"kind": "fixed_treatment_count"} yields 4 treatment markets (share 0.13); {"kind": "matched_pairs", "treatment_count": 4} yields 4 (share 0.13); {"kind": "matched_pairs"} with no count yields 15 treatment markets (share 0.50) on every candidate, with warnings=[] — no diagnostic anywhere. The only enforcement of treatment shares in the package is those candidates.py lines; cli/main.py only validates max>=min consistency, so nothing downstream catches the violation. The scenario is realistic (matched_pairs is a documented policy kind and treatment_count defaults to None), and the configured risk cap is silently exceeded by 2.5x. Severity 'major' is appropriate: the paired design is still statistically valid, but a production geo test would treat ~50% of markets against a configured 20% cap with no warning.
verification.suggested_fix: In _policy_context (src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py, matched_pairs branch around line 312), default the pair count to the share-capped default instead of None: `n_pairs = policy_spec.treatment_count or default_treatment_count` and pass `n_pairs=n_pairs` to construct_matched_pairs (default_treatment_count already encodes the min/max_treatment_share caps, clamped to [min_t, max_t]). Additionally, after pairing, compare the resulting treatment_count = len(pairs) against the max-share cap (pass max_t into _policy_context or recompute from spec/defaults) and append a warning such as "matched_pairs_treatment_share_exceeds_max" when len(pairs) > max_t — this also covers the case where a user-specified treatment_count exceeds the cap. Add a regression test asserting that AssignmentPolicySpec(kind="matched_pairs") with treatment_count=None on an N-market universe produces candidates whose treatment share respects effective_max_treatment_share.

bugs.summary: build_supergeos merges an underfilled leftover bucket into supergeos[-1], which can belong to a different group partition (e.g. a different region), violating the declared group_columns blocking.
bugs.failure_scenario: Region A has one large market (volume 5000 >= threshold), region B has two tiny markets totaling 15 < threshold=100 with group_columns=['region']: the region-B leftovers are merged into region A's singleton supergeo, producing supergeo_001 = ('a_big','b_tiny1','b_tiny2') spanning both regions. Any experiment randomized on these units treats/holds out cross-region bundles the user explicitly asked to keep separate, and the giant merged unit distorts unit-level estimators.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: supergeo_001 ('a_big', 'b_tiny1', 'b_tiny2') 5015.0 {'carried_underfilled': True}. Code: 'if supergeos and bucket_volume < threshold: previous = supergeos[-1]' — supergeos is the global list across all group partitions, and previous may be a large singleton or from another group.

verification.explanation: The defect is real and reproduced. In build_supergeos (src/fieldtrial/design/supergeo.py), the leftover-bucket merge at line 83 checks only `if supergeos and bucket_volume < threshold`, where `supergeos` is the global accumulator spanning all group partitions. When a partition's markets are all below threshold and sum below threshold, that partition emits no supergeo before the leftover branch runs, so `supergeos[-1]` belongs to the previous partition and the merge crosses the group boundary — violating the group_columns blocking the docstring promises ("grouped within stable metadata partitions"). Reproduction with region A = one 5000-volume market and region B = two markets totaling 15, min_volume=100, group_columns=['region'] yields a single unit supergeo_001 ('a_big', 'b_tiny1', 'b_tiny2') 5015.0 with carried_underfilled=True, exactly matching the auditor's evidence. The scenario is reachable in production: candidates.py line 269 calls build_supergeos with policy-specified group_columns and uses the resulting supergeos directly as randomization units, so an experiment would treat/hold out a cross-region bundle the user asked to keep separate. No test expects cross-group merging.
verification.suggested_fix: Restrict the leftover merge to supergeos created within the current group partition. At the top of the `for _, group in groups:` loop record `group_start = len(supergeos)`, and change the merge condition at line 83 from `if supergeos and bucket_volume < threshold:` to `if len(supergeos) > group_start and bucket_volume < threshold:`. Then an underfilled group with no prior supergeo in its own partition falls into the else branch and is emitted as its own supergeo with carried_underfilled=True (and under_min_volume=True), preserving the group_columns blocking. Add a regression test: one large market in region A plus tiny markets in region B whose total is below min_volume must yield two supergeos, neither spanning both regions.

bugs.summary: For fixed_treatment_count designs the stored control set is a volume-stratified RNG subsample of the complement, but the persisted assignment policy declares controls = complement, so the declared randomization space does not match the actual mechanism and downstream randomization p-values are not faithful to the design.
bugs.failure_scenario: 30 eligible markets, defaults: every candidate has 4 treatment and only 15 of the 26 complement markets as controls (volume-stratified subsample dependent on rng). The persisted assignment_policy payload is plain fixed_treatment_count with no record of the control-selection rule. At analysis time _assignment_policy_from_completed_spec (inference/orchestration.py:1179) reconstructs a fixed-count policy over the 19 *used* geos only; the randomization test then permutes treatment uniformly over 4-subsets of 19, which is neither the design-time draw (4 from 30) nor the correct conditional distribution given the volume-stratified control selection. Combined with registry blocks and interference-based control exclusion also applied outside the policy, randomization p-values are computed under a different assignment distribution than the one that generated the data.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: 'T: 4 C: 15 complement: 26 controls==complement: False; declared policy kind: fixed_treatment_count'. Code: _controls_from_assignment subsamples via _select_controls (volume strata + rng) for non-matched/stratified/supergeo kinds; orchestration rebuilds AssignmentPolicy(markets=design.all_geos, treatment_count=len(treatment_geos)).

verification.explanation: Confirmed by code reading and execution. Design time: in CandidateGenerator._controls_from_assignment (src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py:469), for fixed_treatment_count/candidate_constrained kinds the stored control set is a volume-stratified RNG subsample of the treatment complement via _select_controls (strata by volume, rng.permutation, capped at min_controls*3), while the persisted assignment_policy payload (candidates.py:178, built at _policy_context lines 344-356) is just the declared AssignmentPolicySpec dump plus an execution block that records only strata/matched-pairs info — nothing about the control-selection rule, pool, or seed. Reproduced with pure defaults on a 30-market panel: every candidate has T=4, C=15 of the 26-market complement, control sets differ per candidate (RNG-dependent), declared policy kind is plain fixed_treatment_count; across 40 draws all 30 markets appear as treatment, so the design-time treatment space is 4-subsets of 30 (C(30,4)=27,405). Analysis time: _assignment_policy_from_completed_spec (src/fieldtrial/inference/orchestration.py:1166-1189) rebuilds AssignmentPolicy(markets=design.all_geos, treatment_count=len(treatment_geos)) where all_geos is the 19 used geos only; reproduced n_feasible=3,876=C(19,4). _run_randomization_inference then permutes treatment uniformly over 4-subsets of the 19 used geos — neither the design draw (uniform 4-of-30, further filtered by registry blocks and rerandomization thresholds applied outside the policy) nor the correct conditional distribution given volume-stratified control selection (conditional on the union set, assignments are not exchangeable because controls were chosen by volume strata). This directly contradicts the randomization module's stated guarantee of preserving the assignment mechanism ("assignment-aware" Fisher inference), so randomization p-values are computed under a different assignment distribution than the one that generated the data. Reachable with all defaults (default policy kind is fixed_treatment_count; subsampling triggers whenever the complement exceeds 3*min_control_markets=15). Severity major is appropriate: no crash and the result is still a recognizable conditional-permutation approximation, but the advertised design-faithful randomization inference is invalid for these designs.
verification.suggested_fix: Make the persisted policy describe the actual mechanism, and make analysis-time reconstruction honor it. Concretely: (1) In CandidateGenerator._controls_from_assignment / generate_for_test, record the realized control-selection mechanism in the candidate's assignment_policy payload (e.g. execution["control_selection"] = {"method": "volume_stratified_subsample", "pool": complement_after_blocks, "target_count": max(min_controls, min_controls*3), "strata_count": ..., "seed": ...} plus the registry-block and interference-exclusion sets). (2) In _assignment_policy_from_completed_spec (inference/orchestration.py), when the completed design's control set is a strict subset of the declared policy's complement, either reconstruct the full mechanism — sample treatment from the declared policy over all policy markets and re-apply the recorded block/exclusion/control-subsample steps per draw, computing the statistic on each simulated realization — or refuse and emit an explicit warning/diagnostic that the randomization p-value is a conditional permutation approximation over used geos rather than a design-faithful test. (3) Alternatively (simplest correct option): stop subsampling controls for fixed-count kinds — return the full post-block complement as controls (the subsample only exists to shrink the control pool, which downstream estimators do not require) — so that declared policy and realized assignment coincide and the existing reconstruction over all_geos becomes faithful.

bugs.summary: n_feasible_assignments for matched_pairs returns 2**len(pairs), ignoring required/forbidden constraints and the treatment_count==len(pairs) requirement, so it overcounts (or reports nonzero for an empty space).
bugs.failure_scenario: pairs=((p1a,p1b),(p2a,p2b)) with forbidden=(p1a,): n_feasible reports 4 but only 2 assignments are feasible — the wrong count is persisted into inference diagnostics ('n_feasible_assignments') and drives the exact-enumeration-vs-sampling branch in inference/randomization.py:177. With treatment_count=3 != len(pairs)=2, n_feasible reports 4 while the space is actually empty; enumerate() returns [] silently and sample() raises ValueError('could not sample a feasible matched-pair assignment') after 100 blind retries.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: 'pairs n_feasible: 4 enumerated: 2' and 'mismatch n_feasible: 4 enumerated: 0; pol3.sample raised: ValueError could not sample a feasible matched-pair assignment'.

verification.explanation: Reproduced both scenarios by running the package. AssignmentPolicy.n_feasible_assignments (src/fieldtrial/design/policies.py:121-122) returns 2**len(pairs) for matched_pairs unconditionally, while _enumerate_pairs and _sample_pairs filter every candidate through _is_feasible_treatment (lines 373-383), which enforces treatment_count, required, forbidden, and fixed-control constraints. With pairs=((p1a,p1b),(p2a,p2b)) and forbidden=(p1a,), n_feasible reports 4 but enumerate() yields only 2 assignments. With treatment_count=3 and 2 pairs (constructible because __post_init__ never checks treatment_count == len(pairs)), n_feasible reports 4 while enumerate() returns [] and sample(1) raises ValueError('could not sample a feasible matched-pair assignment') after 100 blind retries. Reachability holds: inference/randomization.py _policy_n_feasible (line 489) reads this attribute, persists it into result diagnostics ('n_feasible_assignments', line 280), and uses it at line 454 to decide exact enumeration vs raising for max_exact_assignments; from_spec copies forbidden/fixed-control constraints from user specs alongside kind='matched_pairs', so constrained matched-pairs policies arise in normal usage. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: enumeration/sampling themselves apply constraints correctly, so p-values over evaluated assignments remain valid; the defect corrupts a diagnostic count, the exact/sampled branching threshold, and produces a confusing late error for empty spaces.
verification.suggested_fix: In n_feasible_assignments (policies.py:121), compute the matched-pairs count exactly instead of 2**len(pairs): return 0 if self.treatment_count != len(self.pairs) or if any required_treatment_market is not a member of some pair; otherwise take unavailable = set(forbidden_treatment_markets) | set(fixed_control_markets), and for each pair compute its feasible orientations: options = [m for m in pair if m not in unavailable]; required_in_pair = [m for m in pair if m in required_treatment_markets]; if len(required_in_pair) == 2 return 0; if len(required_in_pair) == 1, options = options if required_in_pair[0] in options else [] restricted to that member; multiply len(options) across pairs (returning 0 if any pair has none). Additionally, add a __post_init__ validation raising ValueError when kind == 'matched_pairs' and pairs and treatment_count != len(pairs), so the empty-space policy fails fast at construction instead of via a blind-retry ValueError in _sample_pairs.

bugs.summary: AssignmentPolicy.sample(n) silently returns fewer than n draws when the feasible space has fewer than n distinct treatment sets, and its deduplication makes it sampling-without-replacement while callers request a fixed number of Monte Carlo draws.
bugs.failure_scenario: sample(10) on a C(4,2)=6 space returns 6 assignments with no warning. A randomization test requesting n_permutations=5000 on a small policy space quietly runs with far fewer draws than reported/configured; any consumer that divides by the requested n rather than the returned count gets a biased Monte Carlo p-value. The fallback 'assignments.extend(self.enumerate(max_assignments=n)[len(assignments):n])' extends with a lexicographic slice unrelated to which assignments were already sampled.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: 'requested 10, got 6'. (Marginal uniformity of the fixed-count sampler itself was verified empirically: 20k draws gave counts 3284-3395 across the 6 assignments.)

verification.explanation: The mechanical observation is accurate — AssignmentPolicy.sample(10) on a C(4,2)=6 space does return 6 assignments (reproduced) — but every element of the claimed failure scenario fails against the actual code. (1) No consumer divides by the requested n: the only Monte Carlo caller, randomization_test (src/fieldtrial/inference/randomization.py), iterates the returned assignments and uses the actual count as the p-value denominator (null_statistics.size, lines 516-519) and in all reporting. Reproduction: requesting n_permutations=5000 on the 6-assignment space yielded an honestly reported n_draws=6 / n_evaluated_assignments=6 and p=(2+1)/(6+1)=0.4286 vs exact 2/6=0.3333 — conservative, not biased; so 'far fewer draws than reported' is false (the report is accurate; only the configured request differs, and the request is preserved separately). (2) The scenario is unreachable via the real pipeline: orchestration.py:547-551 only sets n_permutations when n_feasible > max_enumerated_assignments (default 10000) while randomization_samples defaults to 5000, so sampling never runs on a space smaller than the draw count; small spaces use exact enumeration. The other caller (candidates.py:408) uses sample(1). (3) The 'lexicographic slice' complaint misreads the code: in the space<n case enumerate(max_assignments=n) returns exactly `space` items and the slice [len(assignments):n] starts at index space, so it appends nothing; the branch could only add entries if the rejection loop exhausted n*100 attempts without collecting min(n, space) distinct assignments, which is coupon-collector-infeasible for the fixed-count sampler (~N ln N << 100N attempts). (4) Returning all distinct feasible assignments when the space is exhausted is the statistically preferable behavior — it upgrades the Monte Carlo test to full enumeration, and the retained +1 correction keeps the p-value valid (mildly conservative). The dedup/'without replacement' behavior therefore causes no biased inference anywhere in the codebase; at worst sample(n)'s silent shortfall is an undocumented API sharp edge for hypothetical external duck-typed consumers, not a defect with a real failure in realistic usage. A cheap hardening (not required for correctness) would be to document the fewer-than-n return, or emit a warning/metadata flag when len(result) < n.

bugs.summary: _standardized_mean_difference returns None when pooled within-arm variance is zero but means differ, so an infinitely imbalanced covariate is silently dropped from max_abs_smd and passes rerandomization thresholds.
bugs.failure_scenario: A binary/constant-within-arm feature perfectly separating treatment (100,100) from control (0,0) yields SMD=None; score_balance reports max_abs_smd=None for that assignment and _passes_rerandomization skips the SMD check ('if smd is not None'), so a grossly confounded assignment passes a configured max_abs_smd rerandomization gate. The same formula is duplicated in policies.py:_smd (line 405).
bugs.evidence: Repro output: "t=(a,b) smds: {'flag': None} max_abs_smd: None" — the perfectly separated assignment carries no SMD signal. Should return +/-inf or a warning instead of None.

verification.explanation: Reproduced both legs of the claim by executing the package code. (1) _standardized_mean_difference (src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py:874-885) returns None when pooled within-arm variance is 0 but means differ: _standardized_mean_difference([100,100],[0,0]) -> None; the 1-market-per-arm case (variance hard-coded to 0.0 for len<=1) also always returns None unless means are exactly equal. (2) CandidateGenerator._passes_rerandomization (candidates.py:484-496) checks 'if smd is not None' before comparing to the configured max_abs_smd limit, so a balance dict with smd=None passes a max_abs_smd=0.25 gate (verified: returns True, while smd=5.0 correctly returns False). Thus the most confounded possible assignment silently passes rerandomization. (3) The duplicated _smd in policies.py:405 has the same behavior, and AssignmentPolicy.score_balance (policies.py:210-223) drops None SMDs from the 'finite' list: a binary 'flag' feature perfectly separating treatment (a,b)=100 from control (c,d)=0 produced smds {'flag': None} and max_abs_smd=0.707 (driven only by a benign second feature), ranking the perfectly-separated assignment BETTER than assignments with finite flag SMD of 2.83. Reachability is realistic but narrow (constant-within-arm features, binary flags, or 1v1 market designs), and the affected code is diagnostic/gating rather than estimation, so the claimed 'minor' severity is appropriate.
verification.suggested_fix: In both _standardized_mean_difference (src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py:882) and _smd (src/fieldtrial/design/policies.py:405), replace the degenerate-case 'return ... else None' with a signed infinity so the imbalance signal is preserved: `if not np.isfinite(pooled) or pooled <= 0: diff = float(np.mean(treatment) - np.mean(control)); return 0.0 if np.isclose(diff, 0.0) else float(np.copysign(np.inf, diff))`. Downstream this is already handled correctly: _passes_rerandomization's `abs(float(smd)) > limit` rejects inf, the 0.25 warning in _balance_diagnostics fires, and score_balance's max()/sort push inf-imbalance assignments to the bottom. Two follow-ups: (a) rename score_balance's `finite` list or filter with np.isfinite and record a per-column 'degenerate_smd' warning so inf columns are still surfaced; (b) since balance_diagnostics is serialized into CandidateDesign payloads, ensure JSON serialization tolerates inf (e.g., emit null plus a 'degenerate_smd' warning string in the diagnostics dict, while keeping the inf for the in-process gate check). Minimal alternative if inf is unwanted: keep returning None but make _passes_rerandomization fail (or warn) when smd is None while an smd limit is configured and treatment/control means differ.

bugs.summary: Completed-test randomization reconstruction passes roadmap-level required/forbidden/fixed-control market lists into an AssignmentPolicy whose universe is only the used design geos, so any policy referencing a market not in the final design crashes analysis (root cause: design AssignmentPolicy conflates 'policy universe' with 'used geos').
bugs.failure_scenario: Roadmap policy has forbidden_treatment_markets=['g_excluded']; the candidate design (correctly) never includes g_excluded, so the completed spec's geos are g1-g4. _assignment_policy_from_completed_spec raises ValueError('forbidden_treatment_markets contains markets outside policy universe') and randomization inference for the completed test cannot run at all.
bugs.evidence: Repro (mimicked spec/design objects): "raised: ValueError forbidden_treatment_markets contains markets outside policy universe: ['g_excluded']". Design-module anchor: policies.py:56-59 raises on any constraint market outside `markets`.

verification.explanation: Reproduced end-to-end via the public API. `_assignment_policy_from_completed_spec` (src/fieldtrial/inference/orchestration.py:1179-1188) sets the policy universe to `design.all_geos` (only the completed test's treatment+control geos) but passes the completed spec's constraint lists unfiltered; `AssignmentPolicy.__post_init__` (src/fieldtrial/design/policies.py:56-59) then raises ValueError for any constraint market outside that universe. Executed repro: a `CompletedExperimentSpec` with geos g1-g4 and `assignment_policy.forbidden_treatment_markets=["g_excluded"]`, run through `analyze_completed_experiment(panel, spec, estimators=["did"], inference.methods=["randomization_inference"])`, raised `ValueError: forbidden_treatment_markets contains markets outside policy universe: ['g_excluded']` — and with `return_errors=True` produced 0 results, meaning the entire estimator run for the metric is lost, not just the randomization method. The scenario is realistic: a forbidden-treatment market can only appear as a control and the design module's control selection subsets controls (candidates.py `_controls_from_assignment`/`_select_controls`), so correctly generated designs routinely omit such markets. The design module itself silently filters constraint markets to the unit universe (`map_units` at candidates.py:381-384), so the inference-side reconstruction is inconsistent with the design side, and a forbidden constraint on an absent market is semantically vacuous — raising blocks a valid analysis. Severity stays minor: the failure is loud (ValueError with a clear message), affects only specs whose constraint lists mention markets outside the analyzed geos, and is worked around by editing the completed spec.
verification.suggested_fix: In `_assignment_policy_from_completed_spec` (src/fieldtrial/inference/orchestration.py), filter constraint lists to the design's geo universe before constructing the policy, mirroring the design module's `map_units` behavior: `universe = set(design.all_geos)`; then pass `forbidden_treatment_markets=tuple(m for m in map(str, policy_spec.forbidden_treatment_markets) if m in universe)` and do the same for `fixed_control_markets` and `shared_control_markets` (these constraints are vacuous for markets absent from the design). For `required_treatment_markets`, a market outside the universe indicates a genuine spec/design mismatch (a geo that was required to be treated but is absent from the analyzed design), so either keep raising with a clearer message naming the mismatch, or filter and attach a warning to the InferenceResult diagnostics. Longer term, give AssignmentPolicy an explicit distinction between its market universe and constraint scope, or a `sanitize_constraints` constructor, so design and inference share one code path.

modules.module_verdict: The core statistical machinery in estimators-core is mostly right — clustered delta method, Fieller sets, BCa, TBR cumulative variance, and CUPED scaling all check out analytically and in simulation. However, three reproduced result-corrupting bugs make current production output untrustworthy in common configurations: the few-cluster inference guard is silently bypassed in DiD fit(), ratio-metric DiD relative intervals are off by a factor of the denominator mean (~10^4 in a realistic panel) and flow into report consensus, and pre-fit-only covariate screening silently admits post-treatment covariates that absorb the entire effect. Beyond bug fixes, small-sample inference calibration (TBR under autocorrelation at 83% coverage, market bootstrap at 88% coverage with 4 markets/arm) needs tightening before headline p-values from this module should drive decisions.
strengths: The clustered delta-method ratio variance in src/fieldtrial/metrics/ratio.py is algebraically correct (verified: Var(R) = Var_c(cluster linearized residual)/(n*mean(den)^2) equals n*Var_c/(sum den)^2), with a proper Welch-Satterthwaite df and an explicit two-clusters-per-arm requirement.

The Fieller implementation (inference/intervals.py) is correct and honest: standard quadratic coefficients, t or normal critical values, and explicit bounded/half-line/disjoint/all-real set types instead of forcing a finite tuple; PairedIROASEstimator wires the variance of sums as n*cov(pair effects) correctly.

TBR follows the Kerman-style cumulative accumulation: the effect is the sum of post-period gaps and the variance combines n_post * long-run residual variance with the parameter-uncertainty term x_sum' (X'X)^-1 x_sum and a t(n_pre - 2) reference; simulated null coverage was 94.3% under iid residuals.

Market block bootstrap resamples whole market time series (preserving within-market temporal dependence), resamples arms separately, renames duplicated geos so per-geo statistics count duplicates correctly (bootstrap.py _sample_frame), and BCa uses the correct jackknife acceleration formula.

CUPED/ANCOVA is honestly labeled as market-level ANCOVA rather than classic fixed-theta CUPED, uses strictly pre-period covariates, and was verified unbiased with 98.5% coverage of the true cumulative ATT in simulation.

DiD deliberately clusters at the geo level with t(G-1) df, refuses (in intent) to promote CRV1 inference for one-market arms, and ships parallel-trends diagnostics; the estimand_spec / diagnostics / warnings contracts make scale, aggregation, and assumptions auditable throughout.

methodology_issues.area: TBR cumulative-effect inference (tbr.py + inference/intervals.py cumulative_residual_interval / long_run_variance)
methodology_issues.current_approach: Cumulative post-period effect variance = parameter_variance (iid sigma^2 form) + n_post * Newey-West long-run variance of pre-period residuals with Bartlett bandwidth floor(4*(n/100)^(2/9)) (lag 3 at 60 pre-days), t reference with df = n_pre - 2.
methodology_issues.issue: The small automatic bandwidth badly truncates the long-run variance under realistic residual autocorrelation, and the parameter-variance term still uses the iid residual variance, so intervals undercover. Reproduced in simulation: null 95% coverage is 94.3% under iid residuals but only 82.7% under AR(1) with phi=0.6 (n_sim=300) — roughly a tripled false-positive rate for daily geo data, which is typically autocorrelated.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Use Andrews-Monahan AR(1) prewhitening (or a parametric AR(1) correction as in Kerman et al.'s TBR discussion of temporal correlation), a larger/adaptive bandwidth, or compute the exact finite-T variance of the cumulative sum under a fitted AR model; make the parameter-variance term use the same long-run variance. Alternatively aggregate to weekly periods before fitting.

methodology_issues.area: Market bootstrap inference promoted at very small market counts (ratio_delta.py, bootstrap.py)
methodology_issues.current_approach: BCa/percentile market bootstrap intervals and centered bootstrap p-values are promoted whenever each arm has >= 2 markets; only the 1-market-per-arm case is gated to randomization inference.
methodology_issues.issue: Nonparametric bootstrap over 2-6 markets per arm is known to undercover severely. Reproduced: ratio-DiD with 4 treatment + 4 control markets under the null gave 88.3% coverage for the nominal 95% interval and a 13.3% type-I rate at alpha=0.05 (n_sim=60). Production geo tests frequently have exactly this many markets, so headline p-values are anti-conservative by ~2-3x.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Raise the gating threshold (e.g. do not promote bootstrap intervals below ~8-10 markets per arm) and route small designs to the package's own assignment-aware randomization inference or a wild-cluster bootstrap; at minimum attach a calibrated-coverage warning like the DiD few-cluster guard.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Statsmodels CRV1 cluster covariance with a t(G-1) reference, G = total number of markets.
methodology_issues.issue: With the 10-40 markets typical of geo experiments, CRV1 + t(G-1) is anti-conservative, especially with unbalanced cluster sizes or unequal arm splits. The econometrics literature (Bell-McCaffrey/CR2 with Satterthwaite df; Cameron-Gelbach-Miller wild cluster bootstrap; MacKinnon-Webb) and pyfixest itself support better small-G corrections.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Use CR2 with Bell-McCaffrey Satterthwaite degrees of freedom as the default covariance for the native path (or wire pyfixest's wild cluster bootstrap), and record the effective df in diagnostics instead of the fixed G-1.

methodology_issues.area: Covariate screening scoring and post-selection inference (covariates.py, cuped.py)
methodology_issues.current_approach: Forward selection by cross-validated RMSE, falling back to in-sample BIC, accepting a feature when relative improvement (prev-new)/|prev| >= 1% ; CUPED selects covariates on control-market post-period outcomes and then reuses the same rows for the final HC3 inference; CV folds are contiguous unshuffled row blocks.
methodology_issues.issue: (1) The 1% relative-improvement threshold applied to |BIC| is scale-arbitrary — BIC = n*log(rss/n) + k*log(n) can be near zero or negative depending on outcome units, so the same data accepts or rejects a covariate depending on whether the metric is measured in dollars or thousands of dollars. (2) Selecting on the same control-market post outcomes used for estimation is a post-selection inference problem: reported HC3 SEs do not account for selection, mildly overstating precision with few markets. (3) BIC's parameter count in the DiD path includes post-period time dummies that are identically zero on the pre-only evaluation rows, distorting the penalty.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Score improvements on the RMSE scale only (never |BIC| ratios), fix a unit-free acceptance rule (e.g. percent RMSE reduction), restrict the DiD selection design matrix to pre-period fixed effects, and either use sample-splitting/leave-one-market-out honest selection or note the post-selection caveat in the inference payload.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Optional trimming drops the highest Mahalanobis-style influence pairs (scores computed from the observed response/spend effects, with a hard floor of 1.0 in raw outcome units inside _mad_scale and _pre_distance), then Fieller and pair-bootstrap inference are computed on the trimmed sample as if it were the full sample; pairing falls back to greedy nearest-neighbor matching on pre-period levels.
methodology_issues.issue: (1) Trimming on the outcome and then ignoring the trimming in inference biases the estimate and understates uncertainty — Chen & Au's Trimmed Match chooses the trim to optimize an estimator-specific criterion and its confidence interval accounts for trimming; here the Fieller variances are computed from the truncated sample. (2) The max(..., 1.0) floors make influence scores and matching distances unit-dependent: for small-unit metrics (ratio points, thousands of dollars) all scores collapse toward zero and trimming/pairing degrade silently. (3) The top-level result mixes provenance: interval is Fieller, SE and p-value are bootstrap, inside one InferenceResult labeled 'paired_iroas_pair_bootstrap'.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Either implement Trimmed Match's residual-based trim with its accompanying interval, or report trimmed results only as sensitivity rows (the trim_sensitivity artifact already exists) and keep headline inference untrimmed; replace the 1.0 floors with data-driven scales; emit Fieller and bootstrap as separate InferenceResults.

methodology_issues.current_approach: For ratio metrics TBR builds a daily ratio-of-sums series per arm and reports the SUM of daily ratio-point gaps over the post window as a 'test_window_cumulative' effect in 'ratio_points'.
methodology_issues.issue: Summing daily ratio gaps over e.g. 28 days yields a quantity in 'ratio-points x days' that is neither a cumulative count effect nor an average ratio effect; a reader seeing effect_unit='ratio_points' will over-read the magnitude by a factor of the number of post days. The relative_lift (sum gap / sum predicted) is fine, but the absolute estimate scale is misleading.
methodology_issues.recommendation: For ratio metrics report the average daily ratio gap (or a denominator-weighted post-window ratio effect) and label the time aggregation accordingly, or restrict TBR to additive metrics and route ratios to the ratio DiD / linearized DiD estimators.

drop_candidates.method: BlockBootstrapEstimator ratio path (bootstrap.py) as a separate evidence source
drop_candidates.reason: For ratio metrics it computes exactly the same statistic (ratio_did_statistic) with the same market-resampling scheme, the same BCa machinery, and the same centered bootstrap p-value as RatioDeltaEstimator._bootstrap_ratio_did; running both doubles compute and creates the appearance of two readouts from one method. Keep one implementation (RatioDeltaEstimator already embeds it) and reserve BlockBootstrapEstimator for custom statistic callbacks.

The daily ratio-of-sums TBR estimand ('sum of daily ratio gaps') has no clean causal interpretation, duplicates what the linearized DiD and ratio DiD already cover with better-understood scales, and only contributes a warning about denominator stability. Restricting TBR to additive metrics simplifies the estimand surface.

The post-only delta-method difference-in-ratios ignores the pre-period adjustment that is the whole point of the estimator, so it answers a different (confounded) question inside the diagnostics of a DiD-style result; the delta-method machinery itself is sound but the embedded diagnostic invites misreading and adds a failure path (it appends warnings when denominators are small) without informing the headline estimand.

bugs.summary: fit() re-derives the t interval and p-value from the returned standard error and degrees of freedom, silently undoing the deliberate suppression of CRV1/HC3 inference for designs with fewer than two markets per arm.
bugs.failure_scenario: A one-treated-geo design (1 treatment, 3 controls): _fit_statsmodels sets interval=None and p_value=None and appends the warning 'not promoted with fewer than two markets in each arm', but because standard_error and degrees_of_freedom are still returned, did.py lines 104-109 recompute interval=(-3.76, 3.51) and p_value=0.92 from the severely downward-biased single-cluster CRV1 SE. The result carries confident-looking inference that directly contradicts its own warning and the few_arm_cluster_inference_promoted=False diagnostic. Same bypass applies to the pyfixest path.
bugs.evidence: Repro: DifferenceInDifferencesEstimator().fit on a 1-treated/3-control panel returned interval=(-3.757, 3.509), p_value=0.920 while warnings contained 'DiD CRV1/HC3 p-values and confidence intervals are not promoted with fewer than two markets in each arm.' Guard code: did.py 217-234 sets p_value=None/interval=None; fit() 104-109: 'if interval is None and standard_error is not None and df is not None: interval = t_interval(...)'.

verification.explanation: Reproduced by running DifferenceInDifferencesEstimator(backend="statsmodels").fit on a synthetic 1-treated/3-control panel: the result contained interval=(-0.099, 2.378) and p_value=0.061 while warnings included "DiD CRV1/HC3 p-values and confidence intervals are not promoted with fewer than two markets in each arm...". The guard in _fit_statsmodels (did.py lines 217-234) deliberately sets p_value=None and interval=None, but still returns standard_error and degrees_of_freedom in the payload; fit() lines 104-109 then unconditionally backfill interval via t_interval and p_value via t_p_value whenever they are None and SE/df are present, silently undoing the suppression. The _fit_pyfixest path (lines 408-431) has the identical payload shape (nulls interval/p_value but returns SE and df), so the same bypass applies. The resurrected values are computed from the single-cluster CRV1 SE with df=n_clusters-1, which is exactly the anti-conservative inference the guard exists to block, and they also populate inference_results[0] with the inconsistent combination of a non-None interval and interval_type=None. The few_arm_cluster_inference_promoted=False flag never reaches result diagnostics, and no test covers the guard, so nothing downstream can detect the suppression. Reachable in realistic usage since one-treated-geo designs are the named scenario and the estimator runs in the default ensemble.
verification.suggested_fix: In fit() (src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py, lines 104-109), gate the backfill on the payload's suppression flag so deliberate None is respected: e.g. `inference_promoted = bool(fit_payload.get("few_arm_cluster_inference_promoted", True))`, then `if interval is None and inference_promoted and standard_error is not None and df is not None: interval = t_interval(...)` and `if p_value is None and inference_promoted and standard_error is not None and df is not None: p_value = t_p_value(...)` (also fixing the missing standard_error check on the p_value branch). Both _fit_statsmodels and _fit_pyfixest already emit that flag, so this covers both backends. Additionally propagate few_arm_cluster_inference_promoted into the result/inference diagnostics, and add a unit test asserting interval is None and p_value is None for a 1-treated-geo design on both backends.

bugs.summary: For ratio metrics, relative_interval is computed by dividing the linearized count-scale confidence interval by the ratio-scale baseline, producing a relative interval that is too wide by a factor equal to the treated post-period denominator mean (thousands of x).
bugs.failure_scenario: DiD on a conversion-rate metric (conversions/visits, ~9,600 visits/geo-day): estimate is on the linearized count scale (-4.68 units), relative_lift is correctly -0.48%, but relative_interval comes out as (-19,986%, +10,654%) instead of the correct (-2.07%, +1.11%). did.py sets diagnostics['relative_lift_baseline'] to the ratio-scale baseline (0.100) while result.interval remains on the linearized scale, so base.py _derive_relative_interval (lines 96-98) scales by 1/|baseline| without the linearized-to-ratio conversion by the denominator mean. reports/analysis.py consumes relative_interval for the headline consensus, so report intervals for ratio-metric DiD are nonsense.
bugs.evidence: Repro: relative_lift=-0.00484 but relative_interval=(-199.86, 106.54); correct interval computed as interval/denominator_mean/baseline is (-0.0207, 0.0111). Reported width / correct width = 9639.02 = exactly diagnostics['linearized_ratio_effect']['denominator_mean_treatment_post'].

verification.explanation: Confirmed by code reading and runtime reproduction. In src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py, ratio metrics are fit on the linearized count-scale outcome (base.py prepare_estimator_frame: ft_outcome = numerator - reference_ratio * denominator), so both `estimate` and `interval` are count-scale. The point estimate is converted to the ratio scale via linearized_ratio_effect (divide by denominator_mean_treatment_post) before computing relative_lift, but the interval is never converted. did.py line 130 sets diagnostics["relative_lift_baseline"] to the ratio-scale baseline, and since did.py sets neither relative_interval nor diagnostics["relative_lift_interval"], EstimatorResult.__post_init__ falls into base.py _derive_relative_interval lines 96-98, which scales the count-scale interval by 1/|ratio_baseline| only. The result is inflated by exactly denominator_mean_treatment_post. Reproduced with a synthetic conversion-rate DiD (12 geos, ~9,400 visits/geo-day, rate ~0.10): relative_lift = -0.0016 (correct) but relative_interval = (-103.38, +73.24), i.e. (-10,338%, +7,324%); the correct interval (interval/denominator_mean/|baseline|) is (-0.0110, +0.0078); reported width / correct width = 9433.857 = exactly diagnostics["linearized_ratio_effect"]["denominator_mean_treatment_post"]. Reachability of the failure downstream is also confirmed: reports/analysis.py _relative_interval (line 669) prefers the embedded result["relative_interval"] for headline consensus and interval charts (lines 248, 324, 698, 1386-1388), and its own fallback replicates the same buggy 1/|baseline| scaling. Non-ratio metrics are unaffected. Severity "major" is appropriate: count-scale interval, p-value, and relative point lift are correct, but the displayed relative CI for any ratio-metric DiD is wrong by 3-4 orders of magnitude.
verification.suggested_fix:
In DifferenceInDifferencesEstimator.fit (src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py), compute the relative interval explicitly for ratio metrics where denominator_mean is known, and store it so both EstimatorResult and reports/analysis.py use it. After line 130 (diagnostics["relative_lift_baseline"] = relative_baseline), add:

    if (
        info.is_ratio
        and interval is not None
        and denominator_mean is not None
        and relative_baseline is not None
        and abs(relative_baseline) >= 1e-12
    ):
        diagnostics["relative_lift_interval"] = tuple(
            sorted(bound / denominator_mean / abs(relative_baseline) for bound in interval)
        )

Both base.py _derive_relative_interval (line 90) and reports/analysis.py _relative_interval (line 679) already check diagnostics["relative_lift_interval"] before falling back to the baseline scaling, so this single change fixes EstimatorResult.relative_interval and the report headline/charts. (Note: denominator_mean is only bound inside the `if info.is_ratio` branch, so initialize it to None before that branch or keep the guard inside it.) A more defensive follow-up: in base.py _derive_relative_interval, prefer scale = relative_lift/estimate when both are finite and estimate is nonzero, since that ratio always maps the estimate's scale to the relative scale regardless of linearization; the relative_lift_baseline branch is only valid when interval and baseline share the same scale. Also apply the same explicit relative_lift_interval pattern to any other estimator that combines a linearized count-scale interval with a ratio-scale relative_lift_baseline.

bugs.summary: The DiD covariate selector screens candidates only on pre-period fit and then includes their post-period values in the treatment-effect regression, so a post-treatment-affected covariate (e.g. spend) is selected and silently absorbs the entire treatment effect with no warning.
bugs.failure_scenario: A campaign raises both spend and sales; the user passes 'spend' as covariate_columns. Spend predicts pre-period sales, so select_covariates keeps it (evaluation_mask is pre-only and cannot detect post-treatment endogeneity). The final TWFE regression then conditions on post-period spend, a mediator: reproduced true per-period effect 20.0, plain DiD estimated 18.97, DiD-with-spend estimated -1.56 with zero warnings. This is the classic bad-control bias and it is silent — diagnostics report spend as 'selected' as if that were a success.
bugs.evidence: Repro output: 'plain DiD: 18.97', 'DiD with spend covariate (selected?): [spend]', 'DiD with spend covariate estimate: -1.56', 'any warning about post-treatment covariates?: []'. Selection evaluation_mask=selection_frame[PERIOD_COL]=='pre' (did.py:308) while _fit_statsmodels includes covariate_features over all rows including post (did.py:176-189).

verification.explanation: Confirmed by code reading and runtime reproduction. In src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py, _select_covariates screens candidates with evaluation_mask=selection_frame[PERIOD_COL]=='pre' (line 308), so select_covariates (covariates.py:134-138) scores only pre-period predictive fit; a post-treatment mediator like spend is maximally predictive pre-period and is guaranteed to be selected. The final TWFE regressions (_fit_statsmodels, did.py:176-189; _fit_pyfixest, did.py:387-388) then include the covariate over ALL rows including post-period treated rows, so it absorbs the treatment effect. Reproduced with a 7-geo panel where a campaign raises spend by 10 and sales respond 2.0*spend (true per-period effect 20.0): plain DiD estimated 21.64; DiD with covariate_columns=['spend'] selected spend ('selected_source_columns': ['spend']) and estimated -0.0 with warnings=[] — the entire effect silently absorbed. No guard exists: the DiD method metadata (methods.py:383-408) lists no covariate-exogeneity assumption or post-treatment-leakage failure mode (CUPED's metadata does, methods.py:540), and docs/methodology.md:80-85 affirmatively claims the selector drops 'harmful' features, which it cannot do for the classic bad control. The scenario is reachable in realistic usage since covariate_columns is a public constructor and spec parameter and spend is the natural covariate a marketer would pass. Severity major is correct: the headline estimate is completely and silently wrong, but only when the user supplies a treatment-affected covariate.
verification.suggested_fix: Add a post-treatment covariate shift check and act on it. Concretely, in DifferenceInDifferencesEstimator._select_covariates (src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py), after selection, run for each selected covariate the same TWFE regression with the covariate as the outcome (covariate ~ treated:post + C(geo) + C(time)); if the treated:post coefficient is large relative to the covariate's pre-period residual variation (e.g. |shift| > 0.2 * pre-period std, or statistically significant at the cluster level), (a) emit an explicit warning such as "Covariate 'spend' shifts with treatment in the post period; it is likely post-treatment-affected and conditioning on it can absorb the treatment effect", (b) drop it from covariate_features by default with a diagnostics record (status='rejected', reason='post_treatment_shift'), and (c) allow an explicit opt-in flag (e.g. allow_post_treatment_covariates=True) for users who genuinely want to condition on it. Alternatively (stronger identification-preserving option): never enter raw post-period covariate values into the effect regression — use only pre-period covariate information (e.g. per-geo pre-period mean interacted with time fixed effects). Also add "Covariates must be unaffected by treatment (no post-treatment/mediator controls)" to the DiD assumptions and "Post-treatment leakage in adjustment covariates" to its failure_modes in src/fieldtrial/methods.py, and correct docs/methodology.md, which currently claims the selector drops harmful features.

bugs.summary: The guard 'if n_pre <= 0 or n_post <= 0: continue' does not catch NaN (pivot fills missing period cells with NaN, and NaN <= 0 is False), so a geo with no post-period (or no pre-period) rows enters geo_effects with NaN deltas that poison every pair's influence score and the total effects.
bugs.failure_scenario: One control geo's data feed drops out at test start (no post rows). c3 is retained in geo_effects with response_delta=NaN/spend_delta=NaN, greedy pairing matches it (its pre-period levels are finite), np.median over pair effects becomes NaN so ALL influence scores are NaN, and the estimator crashes with the misleading error 'incremental spend effect is too close to zero to form a finite point estimate' instead of reporting missing post data. With trim_fraction > 0, sorting by all-NaN influence scores makes trimming arbitrary; if the NaN pair happens to be trimmed the estimator proceeds with non-outlier-based trimming and the data-quality problem is masked.
bugs.evidence: Repro: geo 'c3' with zero post rows -> geo_effects['c3'] = {'response_delta': nan, 'spend_delta': nan, ..., 'post_periods': nan} and fit() raised ValueError 'Paired iROAS incremental spend effect is too close to zero to form a finite point estimate'.

verification.explanation: Reproduced end-to-end against /Users/wj/fieldtrial-proposal/fieldtrial/src/fieldtrial/estimators/iroas.py. In _geo_effects, when a geo has pre rows but zero post rows, grouped.pivot() produces a row for that geo with NaN in the ('response_sum','post'), ('spend_sum','post'), and ('n_periods','post') cells (the columns exist because other geos have post data, so the KeyError guard at line 286 never fires). float(NaN) succeeds, and the guard at line 288 `if n_pre <= 0 or n_post <= 0: continue` is False for NaN, so the geo is retained. Demonstrated: geo 'c3' with no post rows yields geo_effects['c3'] = {'response_delta': nan, 'spend_delta': nan, 'pre_response_per_period': 91.0, 'pre_spend_per_period': 50.0, 'post_periods': nan}. The scenario is reachable in realistic usage: _prepare_frame only requires the overall frame to contain both periods, not per-geo coverage, and greedy pairing (_resolve_pairs/_pre_distance) uses only the finite pre-period levels, so c3 gets matched (pairs came out [('t1','c1'),('t2','c2'),('t3','c3')]). In _pair_effects, np.median over the arrays containing the NaN pair makes ALL influence scores NaN. With trim_fraction=0, fit() raised exactly the claimed misleading error: ValueError 'Paired iROAS incremental spend effect is too close to zero to form a finite point estimate' (the NaN sum makes _safe_ratio non-finite; the real problem is missing post data). The masking branch was also verified: with trim_fraction=0.34 and the NaN pair sorted into the trimmed head (sorting on all-NaN keys is arbitrary — NaN comparisons are all False so stable sort preserves input order), fit() succeeded with estimate 3.0 and the NaN pair was reported as 'trimmed as high-influence pair-level outliers', silently masking the data-quality problem and making the trim non-outlier-based. Severity 'major' is appropriate: the common outcome is a loud but misleading crash, and the trim>0 outcome mislabels a data-quality drop as statistical outlier trimming; the point estimate itself is not silently corrupted.
verification.suggested_fix: In _geo_effects (src/fieldtrial/estimators/iroas.py line 288), make the guard NaN-aware by inverting it: replace `if n_pre <= 0 or n_post <= 0: continue` with `if not (n_pre > 0 and n_post > 0): continue` (NaN > 0 is False, so geos missing either period are skipped). For robustness against NaN sums with nonzero period counts, prefer: `if not (n_pre > 0 and n_post > 0 and np.isfinite(response_pre) and np.isfinite(response_post) and np.isfinite(spend_pre) and np.isfinite(spend_post)): continue`. Verified by monkeypatched simulation: with NaN geos excluded, the same panel fits cleanly (estimate 3.0) and existing paths handle the exclusion — greedy pairing skips the geo (with the 'controls were exhausted' warning) and explicit pairings raise the clear "Pair ... is missing data" error. Optionally, collect the skipped geo ids and surface them in fit() warnings/diagnostics (e.g., "geos dropped for missing pre/post data: [...]") so the data-quality issue is reported explicitly rather than inferred.

modules.module_verdict: The weight-fitting cores are strong — SDID is a faithful Algorithm-1 port and ridge ASCM matches Ben-Michael et al. — and the inference philosophy (conformal/placebo instead of fake Wald SEs, joint predictive simulation for the state-space method) is sound and empirically well calibrated for SC/SDID/ASCM. However, three reproduced result-corrupting defects make parts of this module unsafe for production readouts: the forecast estimator's quadratic-trend normalization bug fabricates double-digit lifts with tiny p-values on clean trending data, SDID reports a cumulative-scale interval around a per-period-average estimate (the estimate falls outside its own CI), and the MC-NNM implementation omits Athey et al.'s unpenalized fixed effects, yielding systematically positive null estimates and a ~50% false-positive rate under an iid null. Fix those three plus the cross-estimator estimand-scale inconsistency before trusting absolute estimates or forecast/matrix-completion significance calls.
strengths: The native SDID weight fitting is a genuinely faithful port of the synthdid R package's Algorithm 1: collapsed form, column-demeaning intercepts, Frank-Wolfe steps with eta = n_rows*zeta^2, zeta_omega = (N1*T1)^{1/4}*sigma with sigma from first differences of control pre outcomes, two-stage fit with max(v)/4 sparsification, and min_decrease^2 stopping — all verified line-by-line against the reference, and the point estimate recovers a known effect in reproduction (5.59 vs true 5.0).

The ridge ASCM adjustment matches Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein's weight-space formulation exactly (donor-demeaned imbalance times (X0'X0 + lambda I)^{-1} X0), with the adjustment provably summing to zero so weights stay affine; extrapolation diagnostics (negative coefficients, L1 norm, correction magnitude) are surfaced with warnings, and lambda is selected by leave-one-pre-period-out CV as the paper suggests.
Empirical null calibration of the conformal inversion is good where the residual path is coherent: 20-seed null false-positive rates were 1/20 (SC), 0/20 (SDID), 1/20 (ASCM) at alpha=0.05.

The explicit standard-error policy — refusing to report parametric SEs derived from in-sample pre-period residuals for SCM-family methods, and saying so in diagnostics — is more honest than most industry tooling.

The state-space estimator answers the joint-vs-per-period question correctly: cumulative-effect intervals come from joint predictive simulation over the whole post window (verified per-horizon simulation SDs match statsmodels forecast SEs), with an AR(1)-correlated multivariate-normal fallback rather than naive per-period interval aggregation.
Pre-fit quality gating for native SCM (max_pre_rmse_ratio, treated-outside-donor-range share) refuses to report estimates from degenerate fits instead of silently returning garbage.

Honest optional-backend boundaries: explicit backend modes fail loudly, auto fallbacks are labeled native_fallback with visible warnings, and the native SDID is explicitly not reported as the external synthdid package.

Family-aware consensus uses one representative relative lift per independent evidence family and never averages raw estimates across methods — the right call given the (currently inconsistent) absolute scales.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Soft-impute nuclear-norm shrinkage applied directly to the raw outcome matrix; unit/time structure only enters via the row+column-mean initial fill, which is then overwritten by shrunk SVD iterations.
methodology_issues.issue: Athey, Bayati, Doudchenko, Imbens & Khosravi (2021) estimate Y = L + Gamma 1' + 1 Delta' + eps with UNPENALIZED two-way fixed effects and shrink only L. Penalizing the level means the largest singular component absorbs shrinkage, and the bias lands disproportionately in the imputed treated-post cells. Reproduced: systematic positive null bias and 50% false-positive rate (see bugs).
methodology_issues.recommendation: Estimate row/column fixed effects without shrinkage (alternate between two-way demeaning on observed cells and soft-impute on the residual matrix), matching Athey et al.'s objective; re-run the null calibration suite afterwards and add a null-FPR regression test for matrix_completion.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Only conformal test inversion on the lambda-adjusted pre/post gap path; no standard error; placebo path unimplemented (dead code).
methodology_issues.issue: Arkhangelsky et al. (2021) specify jackknife (Algorithm 3) and placebo (Algorithm 4) variance estimators for SDID; the package implements neither despite the docstring claiming Algorithm-1 fidelity 'with jackknife/placebo' family conventions. The conformal interval was empirically well calibrated in an iid null (0/20 false positives) but is on the wrong scale for the reported estimate (see bug) and yields no SE for downstream portfolio covariance penalties, which expect standard errors.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Implement Algorithm 4 placebo variance (resample size-N1 placebo treated sets from controls, recompute tau) when N0 >> N1, and jackknife over units when N1 > 1; report SE and a t-style interval on the same per-period-average scale as the estimate, keeping conformal as a secondary readout.

methodology_issues.area: Conformal inference on stacked unit-time residuals (matrix completion / GSC)
methodology_issues.current_approach: Treated pre residuals for all treated units are flattened row-major into one vector, post gaps likewise; conformal_counterfactual_test_inversion then does circular moving-block permutations over the concatenation as if it were a single time series.
methodology_issues.issue: Blocks wrap across unit boundaries, so the 'moving block' no longer corresponds to any temporal structure; block exchangeability (Chernozhukov-Wuthrich-Zhu) is justified for a single counterfactual path, not for a concatenation of units where residuals share within-unit level errors and cross-unit independence. The observed 'block' is the only one mixing all units' post windows while scanned blocks are mostly within-unit, distorting the reference distribution.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Run the conformal inversion on the treated-average (or treated-sum) time series so pre residuals and post gaps form one coherent path per time period, as done for synthetic_control/SDID; or use within-unit blocks with a sum-over-units statistic.

methodology_issues.current_approach: synthetic_control/ASCM placebos fit each single control as pseudo-treated and compare its cumulative gap to the gap of the MEAN of n_treated markets; matrix completion compares single-row placebo sums to an n_treated-row sum.
methodology_issues.issue: The placebo null draws are not on the scale of the treated statistic: the treated-mean series has ~1/n_treated the noise variance of a single market, so SC/ASCM placebo intervals are conservative for n_treated>1, while MC's are anti-conservative (filed as bug). Abadie-style placebo tests also recommend discarding placebo units whose pre-fit RMSE is much worse than the treated fit; no such gating is applied, so poorly-fit placebos inflate the null spread.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Form placebo draws from size-n_treated resampled pseudo-treatment groups (mean for SC, sum for MC) rather than single units, and drop or down-weight placebos with pre-RMSE > ~2-5x the treated pre-RMSE per Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller.

methodology_issues.current_approach: Newey-West long-run variance of 2-14 holdout residuals scaled by n_post, zero parameter variance, df = n_residuals - n_features - 1.
methodology_issues.issue: Three weaknesses: (1) LRV estimated from as few as 2-3 holdout residuals is essentially noise; (2) parameter_variance=0 ignores trend-extrapolation uncertainty, which grows with horizon and dominates for the quadratic model — anti-conservative for long post windows; (3) the df correction is misapplied to out-of-sample residuals (filed as bug). The state-space estimator in bayesian.py solves this correctly via joint simulation; the ridge forecast should not ship a weaker interval for the same estimand family.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Propagate ridge parameter covariance into the post-window forecast variance (X_post Cov(beta) X_post' summed) or replace the interval with the same joint-simulation approach used in bayesian.py; require a minimum holdout length before trusting the LRV.

methodology_issues.current_approach: MLE-fitted statsmodels UnobservedComponents, joint simulation of future paths at fixed MLE hyperparameters, reported with method_family='bayesian' and a 'posterior_probability' field.
methodology_issues.issue: There is no prior and no parameter uncertainty: simulation conditions on MLE variance parameters, which understates predictive uncertainty exactly when it matters (short pre-periods, weakly identified trend variance) — the regime where users reach for BSTS-style methods. Calling the tail probability 'posterior_probability' misrepresents a frequentist predictive probability. Also no weekly seasonal component by default despite daily geo data (the ridge forecast has weekday dummies; this model does not).
methodology_issues.recommendation: Rename to state_space_forecast (metadata already partially disclaims), add parameter uncertainty by sampling hyperparameters from the asymptotic MLE distribution (or Bayesian estimation via statsmodels' fit_bayes/PyMC adapter) before simulating, and add freq_seasonal for daily panels. Joint-simulation aggregation itself is correct and verified against forecast SEs.

methodology_issues.current_approach: EstimandSpec has outcome_scale/target_population/time_aggregation but no field distinguishing per-average-market from summed-across-markets aggregation; SC/ASCM report treated-mean cumulative effects, MC/forecast/bayesian report treated-sum cumulative effects under identical labels.
methodology_issues.issue: The stated purpose of estimand_spec is to let downstream users detect incompatible scales; it fails for the most basic scale difference in the package (factor of n_treated, reproduced). Consensus survives only because it uses relative lift.
methodology_issues.recommendation: Add a population_aggregation field ('treated_mean' vs 'treated_sum'), set it correctly per estimator, and either harmonize all cumulative estimators to treated-sum (the business-relevant incremental total) or convert at the report layer.

methodology_issues.current_approach: With e_method='all', the adapter takes the pointwise min of lower bounds and max of upper bounds across gaussian/ls/qreg prediction intervals and reports that union as the effect interval.
methodology_issues.issue: The min/max union across three different interval constructions is an ad hoc envelope with no defined coverage level; it is labeled 'conservative' but conflates methodological disagreement with statistical width and inflates intervals unpredictably relative to Cattaneo et al.'s intended per-method guarantees.
