I reproduced the auditor's exact failure scenario by running `render_analysis_report({'results': [{'estimator_name': 'did', 'estimand': 'att', 'metric': 'orders', 'estimate': None}]})` from the package root: it renders without error (25,832-char HTML), and the rendered Estimator Detail row shows `—` for the null estimate. The claimed "RENDER CRASH TypeError" does not occur against the current code; the auditor's R3 repro evidence appears to be stale or fabricated against a different version.
verification.suggested_fix: No fix needed; the None guard already exists at line 851 of the template.

bugs.summary: Bayesian Evidence stats access `item.summary.q05` / `item.summary.q95` unguarded, so a predictive_relative_lift_summary missing either quantile raises UndefinedError and kills the whole report render.
bugs.failure_scenario: A bayesian_time_series result with artifacts.predictive_relative_lift_summary = {'q50': 0.05, 'draw_count': 200} (no q05/q95) crashes render_analysis_report with jinja2 UndefinedError "'dict object' has no attribute 'q05'". The chart-building code (_posterior_density_chart) carefully guards q05/q95 with _finite_float(summary.get(...)), but the template stat cards do not, so the guarded chart path never gets a chance to matter.
bugs.evidence: R4 repro output: RENDER CRASH UndefinedError: 'dict object' has no attribute 'q05'

verification.explanation: Reproduced exactly as claimed. render_analysis_report({'results': [{'estimator_name': 'bayesian_time_series', 'metric': 'orders', 'estimate': 1.0, 'artifacts': {'predictive_relative_lift_summary': {'q50': 0.05, 'draw_count': 200}}}]}) raises jinja2.exceptions.UndefinedError: 'dict object' has no attribute 'q05', killing the entire report. Root cause: analysis_report.html.j2 lines 727-728 render {{ "%.2f%%"|format(item.summary.q50 * 100) }} and item.summary.q05/q95 * 100 unguarded; the Environment in src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py:_template_environment uses default Undefined, and arithmetic on Undefined raises. _bayesian_summaries (src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py:1171) passes the raw artifacts dict through with only a truthiness check ('if not relative_summary: continue'). The scenario is reachable without hand-crafted input: the package's own estimator (src/fieldtrial/estimators/bayesian.py:111) builds relative_summary = {"draw_count": 0} with no quantile keys whenever all relative-lift draws are non-finite (baseline draws near zero), which is truthy and reaches the template — reproduced this too (UndefinedError: 'dict object' has no attribute 'q50'). render_analysis_report also explicitly accepts JSON-like dicts and stored JSON artifact paths, so partial summaries are realistic input. The contrast with _posterior_density_chart (analysis.py:975-984), which guards q05/q50/q95 via _finite_float(summary.get(...)), confirms missing quantiles are an anticipated state the stat cards fail to handle.
verification.suggested_fix: Sanitize in the builder rather than the template: in _bayesian_summaries (src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py ~line 1204), replace "summary": relative_summary with a normalized dict, e.g. "summary": {**relative_summary, "q05": _finite_float(relative_summary.get("q05")), "q50": _finite_float(relative_summary.get("q50")), "q95": _finite_float(relative_summary.get("q95"))}, then guard the two stat cards in analysis_report.html.j2 lines 727-728 the same way the neighboring cards already do: wrap the "Predictive median lift" card in {% if item.summary.q50 is not none %} and the "90% predictive interval" card in {% if item.summary.q05 is not none and item.summary.q95 is not none %}. Also guard line 742's item.summary.draw_count reference ({% if item.summary.draw_count %}...). Alternatively (defense in depth), skip entries with no usable quantiles entirely by changing the 'if not relative_summary: continue' check to also continue when _finite_float(relative_summary.get("q50")) is None and there are no finite draws.

bugs.summary: Score Components table formats `{{ "%.3f"|format(value) }}` without a None/numeric guard, so a None score component crashes the planning report render.
bugs.failure_scenario: A selected candidate with score_components={'priority': 10, 'penalty': None} makes render_planning_report raise TypeError 'must be real number, not NoneType'. Any optimizer path that records an unavailable component as null (rather than omitting it) makes all planning reports unrenderable.
bugs.evidence: R10 repro output: RENDER CRASH TypeError: must be real number, not NoneType

verification.explanation: Reproduced exactly as claimed: calling render_planning_report on a plan dict whose selected test has score_components={'priority': 10, 'penalty': None} raises TypeError "must be real number, not NoneType" from the unguarded {{ "%.3f"|format(value) }} at planning_report.html.j2 line 235; the same payload without the None renders successfully. The path is reachable: render_planning_report publicly accepts raw dict payloads (the package's own tests pass dicts), the normalizer in reports/planning.py line 132 ("candidate.get('score_components') or {}") does not sanitize None values inside the dict, and PortfolioSolution.from_dict/CandidateDesign.from_dict deserialize score_components verbatim from persisted JSON, so a null in a saved artifact crashes the report. One caveat on the scenario wording: no optimizer path inside this package ever emits None (_candidate_score and score_decomposition coerce all components to float), so the built-in pipeline is unaffected — the crash requires an externally built or hand-edited/round-tripped payload. That confirms "minor" as the correct severity. The missing guard is also inconsistent with the template's own conventions: adjacent numeric fields (worst_mde line 201, balance diagnostics lines 218-220) all use "is not none" guards.
verification.suggested_fix: In src/fieldtrial/reports/templates/planning_report.html.j2 line 235, guard the format like the template's other numeric cells: {% for key, value in candidate.score_components.items() %}{{ key }}{% if value is not none %}{{ "%.3f"|format(value) }}{% endif %}{% endfor %}. Alternatively (more robust), sanitize in _candidate_summary in src/fieldtrial/reports/planning.py line 132 by dropping or zero-filling non-numeric entries, e.g. "score_components": {k: v for k, v in (candidate.get("score_components") or {}).items() if isinstance(v, (int, float))}. Note the Metric MDEs chip at template line 228 ({{ "%.1f%%"|format(value * 100) }}) has the same unguarded pattern and should get the same treatment.

bugs.summary: The absolute-path scrubbing regex is a blocklist of specific root directories and misses common roots (/usr, /Library, /Applications, /scratch, /bin, /Sites, ...), so 'scrubbed' public embedded JSON still leaks absolute paths; dict keys are never redacted at all.
bugs.failure_scenario: A warning string 'model cached at /usr/local/share/fieldtrial/model.pkl' or '/Library/Logs/fieldtrial/run.log' or '/scratch/geo/panel.csv' passes _redact_absolute_paths unchanged and lands verbatim in the default (embed_full_data=False) public report JSON, defeating the documented 'scrub absolute paths by default' guarantee. Same pattern duplicated in planning.py:15. Additionally _public_report_payload/_redacted_display_payload only redact string values, not dict keys, so a mapping keyed by file path leaks its keys.
bugs.evidence: R2 repro: all three example paths returned unredacted; regex roots are only Users|home|tmp|var|private|etc|opt|srv|mnt|media|Volumes|root|data.

verification.explanation: Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing end-to-end. `_ABSOLUTE_PATH_RE` in src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py:18-25 (duplicated verbatim in src/fieldtrial/reports/planning.py:15-22) only matches POSIX paths whose first component is in a fixed blocklist (Users|home|tmp|var|private|etc|opt|srv|mnt|media|Volumes|root|data). Running `_redact_absolute_paths` on 'model cached at /usr/local/share/fieldtrial/model.pkl', '/Library/Logs/fieldtrial/run.log', and '/scratch/geo/panel.csv' returned all three strings unchanged, while '/Users/jenna/data.csv' was redacted. End-to-end repro: `render_analysis_report({'results': [], 'warnings': [...]})` with default `embed_full_data=False` produced HTML containing '/usr/local/share/fieldtrial/model.pkl' and '/Library/Logs/fieldtrial/run.log' verbatim (the /Users path was redacted), so the leak reaches both the embedded public JSON (`_public_report_payload`, warnings are not in `_SENSITIVE_EMBED_KEYS`) and the rendered display payload. This defeats the documented guarantee in docs/methodology.md:228-230 ("The public embedded report JSON continues to scrub ... absolute paths by default"). The dict-key claim is also correct: `_public_report_payload` (analysis.py:146-159) only applies `str(key)` to keys and `_redacted_display_payload` (analysis.py:162-171) passes keys through untouched — reproduced: a dict keyed by '/Users/jenna/secret/panel.csv' kept its key verbatim while its string value was redacted. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: it is best-effort scrubbing of paths (information disclosure of directory structure/usernames in shareable reports), not credential leakage, and common home-dir roots (/Users, /home) are covered.
verification.suggested_fix:
1) Replace the root blocklist with a generic multi-segment absolute-path matcher so any POSIX absolute path with at least two components is scrubbed, e.g.:

_ABSOLUTE_PATH_RE = re.compile(
    r"(?:"
    r"(?<![\w:/])/(?:[\w.@+-]+/)+[^\s\"'<>]*"   # /any/root/with/subpath...
    r"|(?<![\w:])[A-Za-z]:[\\/][^\s\"'<>]+"      # Windows drive paths
    r"|\\\\[^\s\"'<>\\]+\\[^\s\"'<>]+"           # UNC paths
    r")"
)

(Requiring two-plus segments avoids false positives on bare tokens like "/metric". If single-segment paths must also be caught, keep the existing root list as an additional alternative.)

2) Move the regex and `_redact_absolute_paths` into one shared helper (e.g. src/fieldtrial/reports/_redaction.py) and import it from both analysis.py and planning.py instead of duplicating the pattern.

3) Redact dict keys too: in `_public_report_payload` use `_redact_absolute_paths(str(key))` for the key, and in `_redacted_display_payload` apply `_redact_absolute_paths(key)` when the key is a str (leave non-str keys as-is).

4) Add regression tests asserting /usr, /Library, /scratch, and path-valued dict keys are redacted in the default (embed_full_data=False) output of both render_analysis_report and the planning report.

bugs.summary: The indexed time-series baseline is the mean of ALL bins before start_date, including any washout gap between pre_period_end and start_date, contradicting the report's 'indexed to each group's pre-period baseline of 100' claim and mislocating both lines against the 100 reference.
bugs.failure_scenario: Design with pre-period Jan 1-31, washout Feb 1-28 (promo anomaly at 3x level, deliberately excluded from the estimation pre-period), test Mar 1-14 at the true pre level: baseline computes to 194.9 instead of 100 and the first post-period point renders at index 51.3 instead of 100 — the chart shows a fabricated ~50% collapse relative to the dashed 100 line even though treatment and control levels equal the declared pre-period. `frame[time_col].between(pre_start, end)` plus `pre = pivot[pivot.index < start]` never consult spec.pre_period_end.
bugs.evidence: R8 repro: baseline=194.9 (declared pre level=100), first post treatment_index=51.3 (true=100).

verification.explanation: The claim is accurate and reproduced by execution. In src/fieldtrial/reports/visuals.py, analysis_visual_payload (line 71) keeps all rows in [pre_period_start, end_date], and _metric_time_series (line 967) computes the index baseline as the mean of every bin strictly before start_date: `pre = pivot[pivot.index < start]`. Neither consults spec.pre_period_end. A washout gap between pre_period_end and start_date is a fully supported, reachable configuration: CompletedExperimentSpec (design/specs.py lines 503-504) accepts an arbitrary pre_period_end, CompletedDesign (estimators/base.py line 145) only requires pre_end < start_date, and the estimation pipeline's period_masks (estimators/base.py lines 393-403) explicitly excludes rows after pre_end from the pre-period. Repro (executed against the installed package): pre-period Jan 1-31 at level 100, washout Feb 1-28 at 300, test Mar 1-14 at 100 for both groups. period_masks yields a pre-period of Jan 1-31 with mean 100.0 (correct), but the visual payload returns baseline = 194.915 for both treatment and control, and the first post-period point renders at treatment_index = control_index = 51.30 instead of 100 — exactly matching the auditor's R8 numbers. The report template (reports/templates/analysis_report.html.j2 line 533) states 'Treatment and control indexed to each group's pre-period baseline of 100', so the chart contradicts both the report's stated methodology and the estimation pipeline's actual pre-period, showing a fabricated ~49% level collapse. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: it is a visualization-only defect (estimates and inference are unaffected), it only manifests when pre_period_end is set earlier than start_date - 1, and index_gap (treatment minus control) is distorted symmetrically so the between-group comparison is less affected than the absolute levels.
verification.suggested_fix: In analysis_visual_payload (src/fieldtrial/reports/visuals.py), derive the true pre-period end and thread it into _metric_time_series: `pre_end = pd.Timestamp(spec.pre_period_end).normalize() if getattr(spec, "pre_period_end", None) is not None else start - pd.Timedelta(days=1)`, then add a `pre_end: pd.Timestamp` keyword to _metric_time_series and compute the baseline only from data inside the declared pre-period. The most robust variant excludes washout rows before binning so weekly bins cannot straddle the gap: inside _metric_time_series, compute the baseline from a pivot built on `work[work[time_col] <= pre_end]` (replacing `pre = pivot[pivot.index < start]` at line 967), while still plotting all points. Optionally, label bins in the (pre_end, start) gap with period "gap" (or omit them) instead of "pre" so the washout window is visually distinguishable, matching how period_masks in estimators/base.py drops those rows from estimation.

bugs.summary: ±Infinity values in result payloads pass _jsonable (which only maps NaN to None) and Jinja's tojson, producing 'Infinity' tokens that make the embedded report JSON syntactically invalid for browsers and strict parsers.
bugs.failure_scenario: A loaded artifact with interval [-Infinity, 3.5] (Python's json.loads accepts Infinity, and unbounded ratio/Fieller-style payloads are a plausible source) renders a report whose <script id="fieldtrial-report-data"> block contains `-Infinity`; JSON.parse in any browser and any strict JSON parser rejects the whole embedded payload, breaking downstream consumers of the public report data. Chart math is protected by math.isfinite, but serialization is not.
bugs.evidence: R7 repro: embedded JSON contains Infinity=True, strict parse fails on '-Infinity'.

verification.explanation: Reproduced end-to-end. _jsonable (src/fieldtrial/estimators/base.py:45-62) maps only NaN to None; ±inf floats pass through, and Jinja's tojson (json.dumps with default allow_nan=True) emits the non-standard '-Infinity' token into the <script id="fieldtrial-report-data"> block (analysis_report.html.j2:1035; same pattern in planning_report.html.j2:415). The scenario is realistic, not hypothetical: the package itself generates infinite interval bounds by design for one-sided alternatives (inference/intervals.py:264-267 returns (float('-inf'), x) for alternative='less'; inference/orchestration.py:738-742 likewise), EstimatorResult.__post_init__ sanitizes relative_interval via _finite_interval but leaves interval untouched, and the artifact round-trip works because json.dumps writes 'Infinity' and load_analysis_payload's json.loads (reports/analysis.py:80) accepts it back. Repro: rendering a report from an EstimatorResult with interval=(-inf, 3.5), both in-memory and via a JSON artifact file, produced an embedded payload containing '-Infinity' that a strict RFC 8259 parser (and therefore browser JSON.parse) rejects wholesale. Severity 'minor' is correct: the HTML report itself renders fine (the script tag is inert application/json and chart math is guarded by math.isfinite/_finite_float); only machine consumers of the embedded payload break — though that payload is an explicitly advertised contract in the report footer. Minor location nit: the script tag is at template line 1035, not 686.
verification.suggested_fix: Make _jsonable strip all non-finite floats, consistent with its existing NaN handling: in src/fieldtrial/estimators/base.py change the np.floating branch to `return float(value) if np.isfinite(value) else None` and the plain-float check from `math.isnan(value)` to `not math.isfinite(value)`. This keeps one-sided intervals representable as [null, x] / [x, null], which downstream code already treats as missing via _finite_float/_finite_interval. Additionally (defense in depth), configure the report Jinja environment's json serializer to reject/replace non-finite values, e.g. set policies['json.dumps_kwargs'] = {'allow_nan': False} in _template_environment so any future non-finite leak fails loudly at render time instead of emitting invalid JSON. If preserving unbounded-interval semantics in the embedded payload matters, map ±inf to string sentinels ("inf"/"-inf") instead of None and document that in the payload schema.

bugs.summary: New counterfactual-panel code computes total_units = cumulative_gap x n_treatment_markets with no metric-kind guard, which is meaningless for ratio-metric counterfactual paths (sums of daily rate gaps scaled by market count) and assumes the path is on the treated-mean scale for all estimators.
bugs.failure_scenario: A synthetic-control result on a conversion-rate (ratio) metric exposes artifacts['counterfactual'] as a treated ratio path; _counterfactual_chart sums daily rate gaps over the post period and multiplies by n_markets, yielding a bogus 'total_units'-style number in the payload (e.g., 0.002/day rate gap x 14 days x 20 markets = 0.56 'units'). _impact_summary correctly branches on metric_kind, but this chart payload does not; if the pending template update renders total_units for ratio metrics it will print a nonsense incremental-units figure.
bugs.evidence: Code: `if cumulative and n_markets: total_units = cumulative[-1][1] * n_markets` with no metric_kind check; counterfactual artifacts are the 'canonical treated-mean' per docs, which for ratio metrics is a rate, not units.

verification.explanation:
The claim is real, reachable, and I reproduced it end-to-end; note the working tree was concurrently patched during this audit cycle (analysis.py mtime Jul 4 08:58, after my initial read), so the defect is confirmed against the audited version and is already fixed on disk.

Evidence for the audited version: my initial read of /Users/wj/fieldtrial-proposal/fieldtrial/src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py showed exactly the cited code in _counterfactual_chart — `n_markets = _n_treatment_markets(design, [result]); if cumulative and n_markets: total_units = cumulative[-1][1] * n_markets` — with no metric_kind guard, and the template (src/fieldtrial/reports/templates/analysis_report.html.j2) rendered `Total ({{ chart.n_markets }} markets) {{ "%+.0f"|format(chart.total_units) }}` whenever total_units was not None. So the claim actually understates one point: the template render was already live, not "pending".

Reachability reproduced: SyntheticControlEstimator accepts ratio metrics (numerator/denominator). Its _build_series computes the treated path as the treated-market mean of per-market daily rates (synthetic_control.py lines 755-785), and it emits artifacts['counterfactual'] with rate-scale gaps regardless of metric kind (lines 196-226); estimand outcome_scale is 'cumulative_ratio_points'. build-context passes all normalized results to _counterfactual_charts, so ratio-metric panels are produced. Running a conversion-rate panel (5 treated markets, 14 post days, true +0.002 rate lift, ~1000 sessions/day/market) through the estimator gave post gaps ~0.00204/day and chart cumulative_effect = 0.0306 rate points per market. Under the pre-fix line, total_units = 0.0306 x 5 = 0.153, which the template would render as "Total (5 markets) +0" — a meaningless figure, while the true incremental conversions were ~140. Meanwhile _impact_summary (lines 1357-1431) correctly branches on metric_kind and reports rates/numerator_units for ratio metrics, confirming the internal inconsistency the auditor described.

Current state: the patched code now reads metric_kind from diagnostics.observed.metric_kind and only computes total_units when metric_kind == "count"; my live run confirms total_units is None for the ratio metric, and the template now keys off total_units_label with the same None guard. Severity minor is correct: it is a display-level nonsense stat in one counterfactual-panel row; the headline impact summary was already metric-kind-aware.
verification.suggested_fix: Gate the total on metric kind (this is what the concurrent fix now on disk does, and it is correct): in _counterfactual_chart, read `metric_kind = str(((result.get("diagnostics") or {}).get("observed") or {}).get("metric_kind") or "")` and only compute `total_units = cumulative_effect * n_markets` when `metric_kind == "count"`, leaving total_units None for ratio (and unknown-kind) metrics so the template's None guard suppresses the "Total (N markets)" stat. Optionally, for ratio metrics, surface the numerator-units figure computed the way _impact_summary does (_units_from_lift against treatment_post_numerator) if a portfolio-scale number is wanted in the panel. Add a regression test that runs a ratio metric through SyntheticControlEstimator and asserts _counterfactual_chart returns total_units is None.

additions.method: Google Trimmed Match estimator (residual-objective trimmed M-estimator for paired iROAS, data-driven trim rate, CI by test inversion)
additions.rationale: The package already has matched-pair design machinery (design/matching.py) and a PairedIROASEstimator, and its own docstrings explicitly disclaim that its Mahalanobis influence-trimming 'is not Google's Trimmed Match M-estimator' (src/fieldtrial/methods.py:691-697, src/fieldtrial/estimators/iroas.py:141,155). Trimmed Match is the reference estimator for randomized paired spend experiments; advertisers and agencies are routinely asked to reconcile results against google/trimmed_match, and the trim-rate-selected residual objective is meaningfully more robust than influence trimming for heavy-tailed pair deltas. All the pairing, Fieller, and bootstrap plumbing already exists, so this slots into the existing contract.

additions.method: Budget/iROAS-aware design outputs: required budget for a target minimum-detectable iROAS, minimum-detectable iROAS given budget and duration, and budget-vs-duration tradeoff curves in candidate scoring
additions.rationale: This is the single biggest gap versus industry practice. A repo-wide grep finds no budget concept anywhere in design/power/optimize; power/mde.py computes only outcome-relative MDE, and iROAS exists only as a post-hoc estimator. Yet 'how much do I need to spend, in which markets, for how long' is the first question in every geo-test kickoff, and it is the headline output of Trimmed Match design, google/matched_markets TBRMM preanalysis, and GeoLift's budget calculator, and central to how Haus/Measured scope tests commercially. Without it, the planner can rank candidates by outcome MDE but cannot answer the question media teams actually ask.

additions.method: Simulation-based power integrated into candidate scoring: assignment-policy-aware placebo replays with injected lift run through the actual estimator suite, producing empirical power curves per candidate
additions.rationale: Candidate scoring today uses analytic noncentral-t MDE approximations (design/candidates.py:595-661 calling power/mde.py); the simulation pieces exist but are disconnected — calibration/injection.py does injected-lift recovery and power/placebo.py ships only a 'deterministic signal-detection score' that the code itself warns is 'not a stochastic power estimate'. GeoLift's entire market-selection workflow, and Haus/Measured's design tooling, are built on estimator-in-the-loop simulation power because analytic MDEs are known to be optimistic for SCM/SDID readouts on autocorrelated geo panels. Wiring AssignmentPolicy draws + injected lift + the default estimator suite into candidate scoring (with a runtime budget) would make the planner's MDEs trustworthy.

additions.method: MMM calibration export: serialize completed-test results as lift/ROI calibration artifacts (Meridian ROI-prior format, Robyn calibration_input, plus a generic 'lift ledger' with estimand_spec metadata)
additions.rationale: Nothing MMM-related exists in the package (grep for mmm/media mix returns nothing), but in 2025-26 the experiments-to-MMM calibration loop is the dominant industry narrative: Google launched Meridian GeoX explicitly to feed geo-experiment results into Meridian as ROI priors, and Recast, Haus, and Measured all sell geo tests as MMM calibrators. FieldTrial already stores per-test EvidenceRecord summaries (portfolio/learning.py) and rich estimand/inference contracts, so this is mostly an export/reporting layer — small effort, and it is the artifact a measurement team actually hands to their MMM. High leverage per line of code.

additions.method: Holdout / go-dark design support: one-sided negative-lift power and MDE where treatment means turning spend off (inverse-GeoLift-style)
additions.rationale: Holdout ('go dark') tests are arguably the most common geo test at large advertisers — measuring the baseline you lose by pausing an always-on channel. GeoLift ships 'inverse GeoLift' specifically for this. FieldTrial's MDE math is symmetric two-sided and its injected-lift shapes include 'spend', so most plumbing exists; what's missing is one-sided/negative-lift power semantics and design guidance (holdout fraction sizing). Cheap to add, frequently used.

additions.method: Multi-cell / dose-response designs: k non-overlapping treatment cells (budget levels or channel variants) within one test, with cell-level assignment, power, and pairwise readouts
additions.rationale: GeoLift Multi-Cell and Haus both support testing multiple budget intensities simultaneously, which is how sophisticated teams answer 'what is the marginal ROAS at +20% vs +50%'. FieldTrial's specs have no cell/arm concept (grep of design/specs.py finds none); its assignment-matrix rule blocks overlapping treatment but non-overlapping cells are compatible with the core design. Real value for budget-elasticity questions, but touches specs, candidate generation, the CP-SAT optimizer, and reporting, so it is a large change; many teams get by running cells as separate coordinated tests, which FieldTrial's portfolio planner already supports — hence medium, not high.

additions.method: Holiday/event calendar regressors and day-of-week diagnostics for treated-series methods (forecast counterfactual, state-space) and short-test balance checks
additions.rationale: Donor-based methods (SC, SDID, TBR, DiD) largely absorb weekly seasonality through controls, the forecast estimator already has weekday terms (estimators/forecast.py include_weekday), and the panel supports arbitrary frequency — so the core is fine. But there is zero holiday handling anywhere (grep returns nothing), and holiday weeks are the classic way short geo tests and forecast-only counterfactuals go wrong. A pluggable event-calendar regressor plus a 'test window crosses major holiday' design warning is a modest, genuinely-used addition; rated low-medium because it mostly benefits the weakest (forecast-only) evidence path.

drops.method: GeneralizedSyntheticControlEstimator (keep MC-NNM, drop the GSC twin) — src/fieldtrial/estimators/matrix_completion.py
drops.reason: The package ships two latent-factor estimators (GSC and MC-NNM) that its own registry maps to the same independent evidence family 'factor_model' (methods.py:586-609), so by FieldTrial's own consensus rules the second one contributes zero additional headline signal. Typical geo tests (50-200 geos, clean block treatment, no missingness) are not where factor-model methods differentiate from SC/SDID, and when GSC and MC-NNM disagree a practitioner has no way to adjudicate. That's 850 lines of maintenance for a method a geo-testing team will essentially never select over SC/SDID/ASCM. One factor-model opt-in is plenty.

It is a second treated-series-only forecast counterfactual duplicating ForecastCounterfactualEstimator: an MLE statsmodels UnobservedComponents fit with simulated predictive draws — no priors, so the 'Bayesian' name is misleading and invites CausalImpact comparisons the method can't honor (the package elsewhere is scrupulously honest about naming, e.g. native SDID vs synthdid). It is already excluded from the default suite. Fold the state-space model option and joint predictive simulation into the forecast estimator as a `model=` choice, or wait until a real CausalPy/BSTS adapter meets the result contract.

Hoeffding-style bounded-mean confidence sequences assume bounded observations arriving as an accumulating sample; a geo test's readout is one aggregate treated-vs-counterfactual series of unbounded revenue/order values, so the machinery rarely applies as stated and inviting teams to coerce geo lift into [0,1]-bounded 'observations' produces false rigor. In practice interim looks on geo tests are handled with a small number of planned looks and honest 'this is a peek' labeling — which the package already supports via planned-look/alpha-spending metadata in specs. Keep that; drop the e-value/confidence-sequence layer.

drops.method: Learning-value bonuses and empirical-Bayes evidence pooling in the portfolio objective — src/fieldtrial/portfolio/learning.py plus the learning terms in objectives.py
drops.reason: Quantifying the 'learning value' of a future test by empirical-Bayes pooling of prior evidence tagged by domain/channel/season/region, then feeding it as a CP-SAT bonus alongside covariance and overlap penalties, is a research idea, not practice. No geo team can defensibly set these weights, and the risk is false precision: the optimizer's ranking becomes sensitive to knobs nobody can audit in a decision meeting. The genuinely valuable planner core — market capacity, shared-control policy, cooldowns, MDE scoring, calendar constraints, priorities — stands on its own. Demote learning-value/covariance-bonus terms to a clearly-labeled experimental extra rather than a documented headline feature (methodology.md 'Portfolio Optimization' section).

gap_industry.overall_assessment: Coherent, not a grab-bag — but estimator-rich and design-question-poor relative to what practitioners actually buy tools for. The portfolio has a real organizing principle that no OSS competitor has: a market-time assignment matrix with shared-control policy, cooldowns, and CP-SAT portfolio selection for coordinating scarce market capacity across many concurrent tests. That is a genuine differentiator versus GeoLift (facebookincubator/GeoLift), google/matched_markets, and google/trimmed_match, which are all single-test tools. The measurement suite (DiD, ratio-of-sums DiD, CUPED with covariate screening, native SC/SDID/ASCM, TBR, paired iROAS, conformal/randomization/Fieller inference) maps well onto the union of industry methods, the family-aware consensus correctly avoids counting five DiD-flavored numbers as five signals, and the honest native-vs-backend labeling (README.md, docs/methodology.md 'Optional Backends') is above industry norms. Geo-level CUPED already exists, so no CUPED variant additions are needed. The weaknesses are: (1) the design side answers 'which markets and what outcome-MDE' but not the spend-side questions — required budget, minimum-detectable iROAS, budget-duration tradeoffs — that Trimmed Match design, matched_markets TBRMM, and GeoLift's calculator all lead with; (2) candidate power is analytic noncentral-t MDE rather than estimator-in-the-loop simulation, which is the trust anchor of GeoLift-style tooling; (3) there is no MMM handoff at all, in a market where Google's new Meridian GeoX and vendors like Recast/Haus/Measured have made experiment-to-MMM calibration the standard loop; and (4) a tail of redundant or speculative components (GSC/MC-NNM twins in one evidence family, a pseudo-Bayesian second forecast estimator, e-value monitoring, learning-value optimizer bonuses) adds maintenance and reviewer confusion without practitioner pull. Trim those four, add the spend-side design layer, simulation power, a Trimmed Match estimator, and an MMM export, and this is a competitive, opinionated offering rather than an academic method museum. Sources: [google/trimmed_match](https://github.com/google/trimmed_match), [Trimmed Match design guide](https://github.com/google/trimmed_match/blob/master/trimmed_match/g3doc/Trimmed_Match_design_colab_guide.md), [google/matched_markets](https://github.com/google/matched_markets), [Meridian GeoX announcement](https://business.google.com/us/accelerate/announcements/meridian-geox-googles-new-open-source-geo-incrementality-solution/), [facebookincubator/GeoLift](https://github.com/facebookincubator/GeoLift), [GeoLift methodology](https://facebookincubator.github.io/GeoLift/docs/Methodology/), [Recast geo-tool simulation study](https://research.getrecast.com/geolift-sim-study).

additions.method: Honest DiD / partial-identification sensitivity for parallel-trend violations (Rambachan & Roth 2023, ReStud; Roth 2022 pre-trend power)
additions.rationale: The package's DiD/SDID/TBR results (src/fieldtrial/estimators/did.py, advanced.py, tbr.py) rest on parallel trends checked only via placebo calibration and pre-trend balance diagnostics (docs/methodology.md Calibration section). Small-N geo panels almost always show some pre-trend wiggle, and placebo pass/fail is binary. Relative-magnitudes and smoothness robustness sets ('how large a trend violation, relative to observed pre-trend deviations, would overturn the sign/decision?') convert that into a continuous, decision-relevant sensitivity readout. This is the single biggest inferential gap: the package can say an estimate is significant but not how fragile it is to the one assumption most likely violated. Fits naturally into the existing InferenceResult contract as another inference payload with a breakdown parameter (M-bar) artifact.
references: Rambachan & Roth (2023), 'A More Credible Approach to Parallel Trends', Review of Economic Studies

additions.method: Asymptotic confidence sequences and betting-based CSs for unbounded metrics (Waudby-Smith & Ramdas 2023 JRSS-B; Waudby-Smith, Arbour, Sinha, Ramdas AsympCS)
additions.rationale: src/fieldtrial/inference/sequential.py implements only a Hoeffding union-bound confidence sequence for bounded means plus Hoeffding e-values. For revenue/conversion-volume geo metrics the user must declare artificial bounds, and the Hoeffding+alpha-spending construction is far wider than modern mixture/betting boundaries — often by 2-3x at realistic look counts, which directly translates into longer tests or false 'keep monitoring' states in the roadmap replanning engine (src/fieldtrial/portfolio/replanning.py consumes these). AsympCS needs only a CLT-style condition, works for unbounded outcomes, and is what enterprise platforms (e.g. Adobe) ship. This is a replace-in-place upgrade behind the existing anytime_valid_confidence_sequence interval type.
references: Waudby-Smith & Ramdas (2023), 'Estimating means of bounded random variables by betting', JRSS-B
Waudby-Smith, Arbour, Sinha, Ramdas, 'Time-uniform central limit theory and asymptotic confidence sequences' (Annals of Statistics 2024)

additions.method: Few-cluster wild bootstrap refinements: Webb 6-point weights, null-imposed (restricted) WCR-t, and CV3/cluster-jackknife variance (MacKinnon, Nielsen & Webb 2023)
additions.rationale: src/fieldtrial/inference/orchestration.py:675 already implements a wild cluster bootstrap-t, which is good, but it uses Rademacher weights only and is unrestricted (recentered at arm means rather than imposing the null). With the 4-10 markets per arm typical of geo tests, Rademacher weights give only 2^G distinct resamples (32 at G=5), producing lumpy p-values; Webb's 6-point weights are the standard fix below ~10 clusters, and the literature consistently finds null-imposed WCR-t has better size. Adding CV3 (cluster jackknife) as the default SE for market-level regressions, per MacKinnon-Nielsen-Webb's guidance, is a small change to src/fieldtrial/inference/resampling.py's existing jackknife machinery. Also worth an explicit warning that WCB is unreliable with very few *treated* clusters (MacKinnon & Webb 2018) with a pointer to the package's own randomization inference — the current <2-per-arm guard is right in spirit but the threshold is too permissive. Small effort, directly on the small-N geo use case.
references: MacKinnon, Nielsen & Webb (2023), 'Cluster-robust inference: A guide to empirical practice', Journal of Econometrics
Webb (2023), 'Reworking wild bootstrap-based inference for clustered errors', Canadian Journal of Economics
MacKinnon & Webb (2018), 'The wild bootstrap for few (treated) clusters', Econometrics Journal

additions.method: Design-based synthetic control: MUSC unbiased estimator and SC-aware treated-unit selection (Bottmer, Imbens & Spiess 2024 JBES; Abadie & Zhao; Doudchenko et al. 'Synthetic Design')
additions.rationale: FieldTrial's core setting — randomized assignment over a small market panel followed by SCM estimation — is exactly the setting of the design-based SC literature. MUSC gives an unbiased SC-type estimator under random assignment with a design-based variance, complementing the existing conformal/placebo inference in src/fieldtrial/estimators/synthetic_control.py. On the design side, src/fieldtrial/design/candidates.py scores candidates on balance diagnostics (SMD, variance ratio, trend difference) but does not exploit the SC structure when choosing which geos to treat; Abadie-Zhao and Doudchenko et al. choose treated units (and weights) to minimize estimator MSE, which is a strictly better objective when the analysis plan is SCM/SDID and pairs naturally with the existing CP-SAT optimizer infrastructure (src/fieldtrial/optimize/cp_sat.py).
references: https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.02196 (Abadie & Zhao, Synthetic Controls for Experimental Design)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.00278 (Doudchenko et al., Synthetic Design, NeurIPS 2021)

additions.method: Partially pooled SCM for multiple treated units with staggered-start support (Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein 2022 'multisynth'), plus a staggered-adoption guardrail in the DiD path
additions.rationale: All current counterfactual estimators collapse treated geos into a single treated aggregate/mean (e.g. the scpi adapter passes a 'canonical treated-mean' per docs/methodology.md) and CompletedDesign assumes one treatment date. Marketing rollouts are often phased across markets, and the portfolio layer explicitly plans overlapping/staggered calendars (src/fieldtrial/portfolio/), yet no estimator can consume a staggered design — analyzing it with the current two-period DiD reproduces the forbidden-comparison bias documented by Goodman-Bacon/Callaway-Sant'Anna. Partially pooled SCM handles few-treated-units-per-cohort settings gracefully and yields per-geo fits (useful for the existing fit-diagnostics section). Cheap first step (small effort): detect multiple treatment start dates in CompletedDesign and refuse/warn; full multisynth or Callaway-Sant'Anna group-time ATTs is the larger follow-on. Rated medium rather than high because the canonical single-launch geo test is unaffected.
references: Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein (2022), 'Synthetic controls with staggered adoption', JRSS-B

additions.method: Reference Trimmed Match estimator for matched-pair iROAS designs (Chen & Au, Google)
additions.rationale: src/fieldtrial/estimators/iroas.py implements paired iROAS with Mahalanobis-influence outlier trimming and explicitly labels it 'not_trimmed_match_residual_objective'. Google's Trimmed Match is the reference standard for exactly this design in the geo-testing industry: it chooses the trim rate to optimize a studentized-residual objective and provides randomization-based confidence intervals that are valid under the pairing, rather than ad-hoc influence trimming plus Fieller. Since matched-pair budget experiments are a core geo workflow (the package already has matched-pair assignment policies in src/fieldtrial/design/policies.py and Fieller sets in src/fieldtrial/inference/intervals.py), adding the canonical estimator would both improve statistical properties and improve credibility with practitioners who benchmark against Google's tooling.
references: Chen & Au (2022), 'Robust causal inference for incremental return on ad spend with randomized paired geo experiments', Annals of Applied Statistics

additions.method: Covariate-balancing supergeo partitioning via MILP/embeddings (Supergeo Design, arXiv 2301.12044; Optimized/Adaptive Supergeo Design, arXiv 2506.20499, 2025)
additions.rationale: src/fieldtrial/design/supergeo.py builds supergeos by greedy volume grouping within metadata partitions (region/state), balancing only a volume threshold. The 2023-2025 supergeo literature treats partitioning as an optimization that balances baseline outcomes and pre-treatment covariates jointly, which materially improves power and reduces bias for small-N designs where individual geos are unpublishable or too heterogeneous. The package already ships OR-Tools CP-SAT (src/fieldtrial/optimize/cp_sat.py), so an exact/relaxed matching objective is a natural extension rather than a new dependency. Rated medium: it improves design quality for one specific (but common) privacy/scale constraint rather than fixing an inference gap.
references: https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.12044 (Supergeo Design: Generalized Matching for Geographic Experiments)

additions.method: Distributional/quantile treatment effects (changes-in-changes, Athey & Imbens 2006; distributional synthetic controls, Gunsilius 2023 Econometrica) and Rosenbaum-style permutation sensitivity for quasi-experimental mode
additions.rationale: Listed for completeness with a deliberately low rating. Geo-test decisions are made on aggregate incremental volume/iROAS, and with 10-50 markets the geo-level quantile process is extremely noisy, so DTE/QTE outputs would rarely change a launch decision while adding consensus-surface complexity the family-aware reporting would have to manage. Similarly, Rosenbaum Gamma-sensitivity mainly pays off in observational designs; when the assignment mechanism is known (the package's primary mode), the existing exact randomization inference already dominates. Only worth revisiting if the package formally supports a quasi-experimental/post-hoc analysis mode, where Gamma-sensitivity plus HonestDiD would be the right pairing.
references: Athey & Imbens (2006), 'Identification and Inference in Nonlinear Difference-in-Differences Models', Econometrica

drops.method: Market block bootstrap estimator in the default suite (src/fieldtrial/estimators/bootstrap.py, included in default_estimators() in src/fieldtrial/estimators/ensemble.py)
drops.reason: Dominated for this use case by two methods already in the package: assignment-aware randomization inference (exact under the known randomization, src/fieldtrial/inference/randomization.py) and the few-cluster wild bootstrap-t (src/fieldtrial/inference/orchestration.py). Resampling markets with replacement at N=5-25 clusters has well-documented severe undercoverage (nonparametric cluster bootstrap needs moderately many clusters), and the registry already concedes it adds no evidence: it is mapped to independent_family='did' (src/fieldtrial/methods.py:428-431), so it never contributes an independent signal to consensus. Recommend demoting it from the default estimator list to an optional diagnostic; keep the block-bootstrap machinery for time-series residual calibration where it is genuinely useful.

drops.method: GeneralizedSyntheticControlEstimator as a separate portfolio entry (src/fieldtrial/estimators/matrix_completion.py:757)
drops.reason: It is a pure relabel: the class subclasses MatrixCompletionEstimator, calls super().fit(), and only rewrites names/metadata ('backend': 'native_interactive_fixed_effects' despite running the identical MC-NNM low-rank completion). It shares independent_family='factor_model' with matrix_completion, so it adds zero identification content while doubling the apparent method count in reports and inviting users to read two rows as corroboration. Either implement true IFE/GSC (Xu 2017: factor estimation on controls plus parametric bootstrap, which behaves differently from nuclear-norm-penalized completion in small N) or remove the alias. As it stands, MC-NNM strictly dominates it because it IS it.

drops.method: Forecast-only counterfactual in the default suite (src/fieldtrial/estimators/forecast.py, in default_estimators())
drops.reason: Not a full drop — it legitimately fills the no-credible-controls niche per docs/method_choice.md — but in the default completed-test suite it is dominated by every other default member whenever controls exist (DiD, SCM, SDID all use the control information the forecast ignores), and the docs themselves label it 'descriptive/limited causal evidence'. Including a weaker-identification method in the headline consensus by default dilutes the family-median readout the Consensus section works hard to keep clean. Recommend moving it to the same explicit opt-in tier as ASCM/MC-NNM/TBR, triggered when the design declares weak controls.

gap_academic.overall_assessment: The portfolio is impressively current through roughly 2021-2023 state of the art and is unusually honest in its engineering: native Algorithm-1 SDID (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021), ridge ASCM (Ben-Michael-Feller-Rothstein 2021), MC-NNM (Athey et al. 2021), Chernozhukov-Wüthrich-Zhu-style conformal test inversion with moving-block scores plus split-conformal fallback, assignment-aware Fisher randomization inference with confidence sets by test inversion, Fieller sets for iROAS ratios (correctly reporting unbounded/disjoint cases), maxT stepdown multiplicity, a wild cluster bootstrap-t for few clusters, scpi-pkg (Cattaneo-Feng-Titiunik) prediction intervals as a properly-labeled optional backend, and — rarest of all — family-aware consensus that refuses to count five DiD-flavored outputs as five independent signals. Placebo-in-time/space calibration and injected-lift recovery match current geo-industry best practice (GeoLift/Google-style backtesting). For the small-N geo panel use case specifically, the identification-strategy coverage is essentially complete; I found no missing estimator family that would change most decisions. The gaps are concentrated in three places: (1) sensitivity analysis — there is no Rambachan-Roth-style honest/partial-identification layer for parallel-trend violations, which is the highest-value addition available; (2) sequential monitoring — the Hoeffding bounded-mean confidence sequence is 2-3 years behind the asymptotic/betting confidence-sequence literature and is the weakest module relative to its importance in the roadmap-monitoring workflow; and (3) staggered adoption — the portfolio layer plans overlapping calendars but no estimator can consume a phased rollout, and nothing guards against analyzing one with two-period DiD. Secondary refinements: Webb weights and null imposition in the existing wild bootstrap (small effort, real payoff at 4-8 markets per arm), the reference Trimmed Match estimator alongside the current influence-trimmed paired iROAS, design-based SC (MUSC) and SC-aware treated-unit selection, and MILP-based supergeo balancing per the 2025 arXiv work (2506.20499). On drops, the portfolio carries little dead weight; the three flagged items are demotions/de-aliasing (block bootstrap and forecast-only out of the default suite; GSC is currently a relabel of MC-NNM rather than a distinct estimator) rather than removals of genuinely dominated science. Net: methodologically current for its core job, with the frontier gaps in robustness/sensitivity and anytime-valid monitoring rather than in point estimation. Key sources consulted: Rambachan-Roth (ReStud 2023), MacKinnon-Nielsen-Webb (J.Econometrics 2023), Waudby-Smith et al. AsympCS (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.10108 for platform practice), Bottmer-Imbens-Spiess (https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/07350015.2023.2238788), Abadie-Zhao (https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.02196), Doudchenko et al. (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.00278), Supergeo design (https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.12044, https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.20499), Google Trimmed Match (https://github.com/google/trimmed_match), GeoLift methodology (https://facebookincubator.github.io/GeoLift/docs/Methodology/).

informativeness_gaps.gap: No GO/NO-GO verdict anywhere. The report never answers 'did this work and what should we do?' Worse, the two sections that gesture at an answer contradict each other: the Executive Readout shows confident headline lifts (conversion_rate +2.45%, orders +5.57%, '100% direction agreement') while the Decision Summary — three screens down, inside a green success-styled box — says status 'not_evaluable' with a blank Decision Value column for both metrics.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: This is the single question a 6-week geo test exists to answer. A VP opening this file sees +5.57% in the first screen and will greenlight a rollout; the fact that every one of the 10 estimator p-values is >= 0.198 and every interval spans zero is never stated in words. The report actively misleads in the first 10 seconds.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Mostly exists. decision_summary.metric_results (framework, margin, status, and in current analysis.py also decision_p_value, uncertainty_status, supporting_estimators) is in the JSON. Needs: (a) a status->verdict->plain-English mapping ('No statistically detectable lift; observed +5.6% on orders is directionally positive but consistent with zero') rendered as a banner at the very top; (b) a fallback path so that when estimands_compatible=false suppresses median_relative_lift (this exact example), the verdict is computed from the per-result intervals/p-values that DO exist, instead of degrading to 'not_evaluable' while the exec chart still shows headline medians from a different code path (_metric_lift_comparison_chart vs _decision_summary in src/fieldtrial/reports/analysis.py).

informativeness_gaps.gap: No plain-language statistical interpretation. p-values are printed as bare numbers (0.3700, 0.7458) four sections deep; 'moving block conformal inversion' and 'welch satterthwaite t' appear as method labels with zero explanation; nowhere does the report say 'not significant at alpha=0.05' or explain that the conformal intervals of [-53.1, 54.4] orders mean 'this method is uninformative with only 8 donor markets'.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: The audience for a geo-test readout includes non-statisticians. Conformal inversion intervals that are 100x wider than the DiD interval look like a bug unless someone explains they reflect honest small-sample uncertainty. Right now the reader must be a methodologist to know whether to trust anything.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Data exists (p_value, interval, alpha, interval_type per result in results[].inference_results). Needs a computed interpretation layer: one sentence per metric ('5 of 5 methods agree on direction; none reach significance; smallest p = 0.198') plus a glossary tooltip/footnote per interval_type.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No dollarized or absolute impact. The report speaks only in relative lift percentages and unitless estimates (0.5193). Nobody states 'approximately +73 incremental orders over the 28-day window (0.52 orders/market/day x 5 markets x 28 days)' let alone a dollar value or cost-per-incremental-order/iROAS.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: Decisions are made in dollars, not lift percentages. 5.57% lift on orders is meaningless without scale: is that 73 orders or 73,000? Absolute impact is also the sanity check that catches unit confusion (the Estimate column silently mixes per-market-per-day DiD effects with synthetic_control's cumulative ATT of 32.19).
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Incremental units are computable today from results[].diagnostics.observed (treatment_post_total=1378, post_difference=4.13/day, n_treatment_geos=5, n_post_periods=28, relative_lift_baseline). Dollarization needs a new input (value-per-unit in the experiment spec) and spend data for iROAS — neither exists in the payload; add optional fields to the design/spec and thread through to the report.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No observed-vs-counterfactual chart front and center. The only time-series visual is treatment-vs-control indexed to 100 — a raw data plot, not a causal readout. The template supports beautiful counterfactual + cumulative-effect charts with credible bands, but only for the Bayesian estimator (bayesian_summaries), which was not run in this artifact, so the section silently vanishes.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: The 'observed vs what-would-have-happened, with a shaded gap' chart is THE canonical geo-test picture (CausalImpact, GeoLift all lead with it). Its absence is the main reason the report feels like a stats appendix instead of a result.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Must be computed. Synthetic control/SDID estimators already fit counterfactual paths internally but do not export per-period series; extend estimators to emit counterfactual/pointwise-effect series into the visuals sidecar (visuals.py currently exports only treatment/control indexed points, src/fieldtrial/reports/visuals.py:918-982), then reuse the existing _bayesian_forecast_chart rendering for any estimator.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No cumulative incremental effect curve with a confidence band. The 'Daily Treatment-Control Delta' bars are pure daily noise in indexed points; there is no running total showing the effect accumulating (or not) over the test, and no CI band around it.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: A cumulative curve is how practitioners judge whether an effect is real and stabilizing versus a few lucky days; it also directly feeds the incremental-units number. Daily delta bars at this noise level actively obscure the signal.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Partially computable: a raw cumulative treatment-minus-control gap can be built from the existing visuals time_series points (raw treatment/control values are there). A proper effect curve with CI band must come from estimators (the Bayesian cumulative chart in the template shows the target); export per-period effect + band per estimator.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No design-quality context connecting analysis back to planning. The planning artifact computes per-metric MDEs (e.g. mde-chips of 3-6%) and balance diagnostics (SMD, variance ratio), but the analysis report never says 'this test was designed to detect >= X%; the observed 5.6% is below/above that, and pre-period balance was good'. Observed power is entirely absent.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: Without MDE context, a null result is uninterpretable: was the test underpowered or is the effect truly absent? This example is precisely that case — plausible lift, no significance — and the report gives no way to tell whether to extend the test, re-run bigger, or kill the idea.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Must be joined/computed. Planning payload (shared_controls_plan.json selected_candidates[].metric_mde, balance_diagnostics) has the data; the analysis payload has none of it. Either carry MDE into the design spec at launch time or accept the planning artifact as an optional input to analysis rendering. A post-hoc MDE from the realized SEs (standard_error exists per result) is computable today.

informativeness_gaps.gap: Validity/diagnostic evidence exists but is buried in collapsed raw-JSON <details> blocks. parallel_trends status (status: 'ok', slopes, gap_std), synthetic-control pre_period_rmse=2.45, donor_weight_concentration=0.30, placebo draws (n=8, placebo_p_value), time-weight fit — all present in results[].diagnostics but only visible by expanding 10 accordions of pretty-printed JSON. The template's Fit Diagnostics and Calibration Evidence sections did not render for this artifact at all.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: These are the checks that tell you whether the estimates deserve trust — exactly the 'design-quality context' a reviewer needs in minute one. Presenting them as raw JSON tells the reader they are internal debug output, not evidence.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Data exists in the JSON payload. Surface as a scorecard: one row per check with pass/warn/fail pills (the CSS and status-pill classes already exist), e.g. 'Parallel trends: OK · SC pre-fit RMSE 26% of observed level: WARN · Donor concentration HHI 0.30: OK'. Ensure _fit_diagnostic_rows actually fires for these artifacts.

informativeness_gaps.gap: Estimator disagreement is displayed but never adjudicated. On orders, synthetic_did says -3.4% while synthetic_control says +13.2% and DiD says +5.6% — a 16.6-point spread — yet the report offers no guidance on which to weight (despite having fit quality, placebo performance, and family metadata that could rank them). The direction-agreement badge silently disappears for orders (direction_agreement is null) instead of flagging the conflict.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: Multi-estimator consensus is this package's core selling point. When methods disagree, the report must say why and what to trust, or the reader just cherry-picks the number they like.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Mostly exists: lift_spread, families, fit diagnostics, and placebo results are in the payload. Compute a per-estimator reliability annotation (pre-fit quality, interval width, placebo behavior) and render a one-line adjudication note per metric; render direction disagreement as an explicit warning rather than a missing badge.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No per-geo contribution breakdown. Nothing shows whether the orders lift came uniformly from the 5 treatment DMAs or entirely from dma_001.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: Single-market-driven lifts are the most common geo-test false positive (local promos, weather, data glitches). Practitioners always ask this in the readout meeting.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Must be computed. The panel data is available at analysis time but per-geo effects are not exported; add a leave-one-out or per-geo delta table/small-multiple to the visuals payload.

informativeness_gaps.gap: Report header answers nothing. Title is the generic 'FieldTrial Analysis Report'; the four stat cards show Metrics: 2, Estimator results: 10, Treatment markets: 5, Control markets: 8. The experiment name ('Completed Synthetic Pricing Lift'), test window, and any hint of the outcome are absent from the header; Experiment Setup is the 4th section at ~30% scroll depth.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: First 10 seconds: which test is this, when did it run, did it work? None of the three is answered above the fold. Counting estimator results is vanity telemetry.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: All data exists (design.name, start/end dates, decision_summary, headline lifts). Replace the stat deck with: experiment name + window, verdict pill, headline lift + interval for the primary metric, incremental units/dollars.

informativeness_gaps.gap: Decision Summary omits its own evidence. The rendered table has only Metric / Decision Value / Margin / Status; margin renders as '0.00%' with no explanation that superiority-vs-zero-margin means 'any positive lift'; alpha is not shown; the decision p-value, uncertainty status, and supporting-estimator list computed in analysis.py never made it into this rendered artifact.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: A decision framework that shows its conclusion but not its inputs cannot be defended in a readout meeting.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Exists in code/payload (the current template at src/fieldtrial/reports/templates/analysis_report.html.j2:377 already has Decision p-value and Uncertainty columns; the example artifact predates it). Re-render examples; additionally translate status enums (not_evaluable, does_not_clear_margin, supported_by_uncertainty) into sentences and explain margin/alpha inline.

informativeness_gaps.gap: Planning report: no MDE-vs-cost tradeoff visualization. 24 candidate designs exist in candidate_alternatives with per-metric MDEs, durations, and market counts, but the report shows them as an 18-row table plus a separate 24-row 'MDE Detail' table. There is no scatter/frontier chart (MDE vs duration, MDE vs #markets) showing why the optimizer's pick is good, and the objective score (2376.93) is presented with no scale or interpretation.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: The whole point of reviewing a plan is deciding whether to trade two more weeks or three more markets for a smaller MDE. That decision is impossible from raw tables, and an uninterpretable score number erodes trust in the optimizer.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Fully computable from existing JSON (candidate_alternatives[].metric_mde, duration_days, treatment/control counts, objective_score, score_components). Add a frontier scatter with the selected candidate highlighted, and normalize/decompose the score using the score_components already in the payload.

informativeness_gaps.gap: No narrative of what the treatment actually was. design carries only experiment_id and name; there is no field for intervention description, channels, spend level, or hypothesis.
informativeness_gaps.why_it_matters: Six weeks later nobody remembers whether 'pricing_lift' was a 5% or 10% price change; reports circulate to people who never saw the brief.
informativeness_gaps.how_to_add: Must be added: free-text description/hypothesis/spend fields on the experiment spec, rendered in the header.

design_problems.problem: Wall-of-tables back half: sections 4-9 of the analysis report (Experiment Setup, Estimator Detail, Evidence Families, Inference Details, Diagnostics, Machine-Readable Consensus) are six consecutive tables/JSON dumps with identical visual weight. The final ~36% of the 10,900px page is literally raw JSON: a 6.7k-char 'Machine-Readable Consensus' <pre> block plus 10 accordions of JSON diagnostics.
design_problems.fix: Move machine-readable payloads out of the visible document (they are already duplicated in the embedded #fieldtrial-report-data script tag — 253KB in analysis, 683KB in planning). Convert Diagnostics accordions into a formatted validity scorecard; merge Estimator Detail + Inference Details + Evidence Families into one hierarchical evidence section; demote raw JSON to a download link.

design_problems.problem: Tables overflow the viewport with no scroll container: at a 920px window the body scrollWidth is 1265px, so Estimator Detail loses its Interval and P-value columns and Inference Details loses interval/p-value/adjusted-p/effect-probability — the page acquires a body-level horizontal scrollbar and the most decision-relevant columns are the ones clipped off-screen.
design_problems.fix: Wrap each table in an overflow-x:auto container (never body-level scroll), set white-space:nowrap only where needed, and move the highest-value columns (lift, interval, p) leftward so they survive narrow viewports; drop or abbreviate low-value columns (Estimand snake_case labels).

design_problems.problem: Color semantics are absent or misleading. The Decision Summary box is hard-coded success-green (border-left var(--green), #ecfdf3 background) even when the content is 'not_evaluable'. In the exec chart, color encodes metric identity (green=conversion_rate, blue=orders via loop.cycle) rather than good/bad/significant, so a non-significant result gets a confident colored dot. Meanwhile green = Treatment in the time-series legend and green = positive in delta bars — three different meanings for the same green on one page.
design_problems.fix: Define a semantic palette: green only for pass/supported, amber for warn/inconclusive, red for fail/negative, neutral gray for identity. Make the decision box color-driven by status. Recolor exec-chart dots/intervals by significance or verdict, not by metric cycle.

design_problems.problem: Executive chart misrepresents certainty: intervals too wide for the axis are clipped with chevron arrowheads at both ends (synthetic_did, synthetic_control rows), which visually read as emphatic arrows rather than 'interval extends beyond the chart'; the shared axis (-7.6%..17.4%) is set by those same uninformative conformal intervals, compressing the meaningful DiD intervals into a small central zone; the zero line is a 1px hairline on a gray pill that looks like a slider control; headline row's band shows cross-method spread while method rows show CIs — two different semantics in identical visual encoding, explained only in a dense caption.
design_problems.fix: Cap the axis domain at a robust quantile of interval endpoints and render clipped intervals with a fade + explicit '±54 units, uninformative' annotation; visually distinguish spread-bands (dotted/hollow) from confidence intervals (solid); strengthen the zero line and label it; replace the caption with a small legend.

design_problems.problem: First paragraph a reader sees is methodology jargon: 'Headline rows use the median of independent evidence-family representative lifts. Their line spans cross-method representative spread, not a confidence interval…' — before any result is stated in plain English.
design_problems.fix: Lead with one plain sentence per metric ('Orders: +5.6% observed, not statistically significant; all 5 methods agree on direction'). Move the methodology caption to a footnote/tooltip.

design_problems.problem: Time-series charts are noise spaghetti: two daily lines over 117 points with heavy day-to-day variance, no smoothing, no pre/post background shading (just a dashed vertical), y-axis with only 3 tick labels, fixed 760x220 SVG, and no month gridlines or intermediate date labels (only first/last date, so the test-start dashed line cannot be dated by eye). The delta bar panel repeats the same noise as ±87-indexed-point bars where the pre/post distinction is a subtle opacity change (0.34 vs 0.82).
design_problems.fix: Add 7-day rolling smoothing (keep daily as faint underlay), shade the post-period region, add monthly x-ticks and a labeled test-start annotation, add more y gridlines, and replace the daily delta bars with a smoothed or cumulative gap line.

design_problems.problem: Raw identifiers everywhere in reader-facing text: metric names (conversion_rate), estimator names in code chips (difference_in_differences), estimands (synthetic_control_cumulative_att, did_linearized_att), statuses (not_evaluable), and inference methods (moving_block_conformal_inversion) all render as snake_case tokens; the Estimate column mixes incomparable units (0.5193 orders/market/day next to 32.1914 cumulative orders) with no unit column.
design_problems.fix: Human-readable display names exist in method_metadata.display_name — use them everywhere with the code name as secondary text; add a units column derived from estimand_spec (effect_unit, time_aggregation); title-case metrics.

design_problems.problem: Empty cells with no explanation: the rendered Estimator Detail table has completely blank Family and Status columns (fields missing from this artifact), and Decision Value is blank for both metrics — indistinguishable from a rendering bug.
design_problems.fix: Render explicit em-dash or 'n/a (reason)' for missing values; hide columns that are empty for all rows.

design_problems.problem: No navigation or hierarchy aids on a ~11,000px document: no table of contents, no sticky header, no anchor links, uniform h2 styling for sections of wildly different importance (Decision Summary looks identical in weight to Machine-Readable Consensus).
design_problems.fix: Add a slim sticky TOC/nav with section links and verdict pill; visually tier sections (hero > evidence > appendix) with size/background changes; collapse appendix sections by default.

design_problems.problem: Projector/print illegibility: 14px body, 11-12px axis labels and table headers, 10px calendar week labels, muted #667085 on white for most caption text (borderline contrast at those sizes), and no print stylesheet (the 5,300-cell calendar and 253KB inline JSON go straight to the printer).
design_problems.fix: Raise minimum text to 12-13px, darken muted text, add @media print rules (hide embedded JSON, avoid page breaks inside cards), and increase chart stroke widths and dot sizes.

design_problems.problem: Planning calendar's market column is 100% blank: all 67 rows (68/68 in the calendar variant) get the hidden-text class because volume-proportional row heights fall below the 18px label threshold, so every market label is rendered invisible (opacity:0) and identification is possible only by hovering each row — useless in print, screenshots, or projected meetings. The 53-week variant is a 2.4MB file with 5,300+ tooltip divs.
design_problems.fix: Guarantee a readable minimum row height with labels always visible (drop strict volume-proportionality or use a sqrt scale / grouped 'other markets' rollup); label the treatment/control bands with test names directly on the chart; cap the default extent to the event range instead of the full calendar year.

design_problems.problem: Planning calendar color coding is ambiguous: Treatment (dark teal #0f766e) vs Control (blue #2563eb) are hard to distinguish at 13px swatch size and for deuteranope viewers; when a market serves multiple states in one week only the highest-priority state is shown, hiding shared-control conflicts — the report's own headline feature — everywhere except tooltips.
design_problems.fix: Use a colorblind-safe, higher-contrast pair (e.g. dark green vs light blue with pattern/hatching for control), and add an in-cell glyph or split-cell rendering when a market-week carries multiple states.

design_problems.problem: Inconsistent metric ordering across sections: Executive Readout lists conversion_rate then orders; Metric Time Series lists orders then conversion_rate; the reader re-orients at every section.
design_problems.fix: Order all sections by spec.primary_metrics consistently, primary metric first.

design_problems.problem: Planning report's Constraint Audit renders solver internals as two raw JSON <pre> panels ('Solver Diagnostics', 'Score Decomposition'), and Score Components tables list raw keys (mde_penalty, control_pool_penalty) with 3-decimal floats and no interpretation.
design_problems.fix: Replace with a short formatted summary (status: OPTIMAL, 729 combinations evaluated, 0 blocked candidates) plus a horizontal-bar score decomposition; keep raw JSON in the embedded data blob only.

whats_good: Family-aware consensus is genuinely differentiated methodology: deduplicating correlated estimators into independent evidence families (did/scm/sdid) with representative lifts, and refusing to pool when estimands are incompatible, is more honest than any off-the-shelf geo tool. Keep the concept — fix the presentation.

The Executive Readout's dot-and-interval rows on a shared percent-lift axis are the right chart form for multi-estimator comparison; with semantic color and a saner axis domain it becomes the centerpiece.
Fully self-contained single-file HTML with zero external dependencies (inline CSS, inline SVG) — survives email, Slack, airgapped review, and archiving. Do not lose this property in the redesign.
The embedded machine-readable payload (#fieldtrial-report-data script tag) plus the visuals sidecar JSON is a clean human/machine dual-format pattern; downstream tooling can be built on it.

Honest uncertainty engineering in the code: the decision logic requires interval + p-value + calibration agreement before calling a result 'supported'; wide conformal intervals are clipped-and-flagged rather than hidden; per-family assumptions and failure modes are listed in the report — rare and valuable.

Clean typographic foundation: system font stack, card/stat components, uppercase table headers, pill/status classes, responsive breakpoints, and aria-labels on SVGs. The bones are fine; the problem is hierarchy and content, not base styling.

Time-series indexing to pre-period = 100 with a test-start marker is the right normalization for visually comparing treatment vs control levels, and the visuals sidecar cleanly separates data from rendering.

The portfolio calendar concept (markets x weeks, volume-weighted rows, pre/live/cooldown/control states with legend and per-cell tooltips) is exactly the right artifact for shared-control planning — it only needs legible labels and conflict rendering.

Warning/status affordances exist and look right where used: amber warning callouts, pass/fail/warn status pills, calibration-failure row tinting, and a 'Things To Check' section that leads the planning report when warnings exist.

Diagnostics depth is excellent in the payload: parallel-trends slopes, donor/time weight HHI, placebo draw distributions, BCa bootstrap internals, conformal grids — the raw material for a first-class validity scorecard is already computed.
report_critique.summary: Verdict as a practitioner: the statistical engine is ahead of the report — the payload contains nearly everything a rigorous readout needs, but the HTML surfaces it as a methods appendix, not a decision document. First 10 seconds (FAIL): I need experiment name, window, a GO/NO-GO verdict, headline lift with uncertainty, and dollarized impact; instead I get a generic title, vanity stat cards (2 metrics, 10 estimator results), and a jargon caption, while the actual answer for this example — 'directionally positive ~5.6% on orders, but zero of 10 results are significant (all p >= 0.198, every interval spans zero)' — is never stated anywhere in words; the Decision Summary even renders 'not_evaluable' in a green success box three screens down, contradicting the confident +5.57% headline dot above it. First minute (FAIL): I need observed-vs-counterfactual and cumulative-incremental-effect charts with CI bands, design-quality context (was the observed lift above the designed MDE? was the pre-period balanced?), and an adjudication of the 16-point estimator disagreement on orders; none exist — the only chart is a noisy treatment-vs-control daily spaghetti, and MDE/balance data sits unjoined in the planning artifact. Deep dive (MIXED): the diagnostics are impressively rich (parallel trends, placebo draws, donor HHI, BCa internals) but delivered as ten collapsed raw-JSON accordions plus a 6.7k-character JSON block occupying the last third of the page, and key table columns (interval, p-value) clip off-screen at laptop width. The planning report shares the DNA: a good calendar concept with 100% of market labels rendered invisible, MDEs shown as 24 raw table rows instead of a tradeoff frontier, and solver JSON dumped into the reader's view. Redesign priorities: (1) verdict banner + plain-language interpretation at top, driven by the existing decision logic with a fallback when pooling is suppressed; (2) counterfactual and cumulative-effect charts for all estimators, not just Bayesian; (3) absolute/dollarized impact (units computable today; add value-per-unit and spend inputs); (4) a validity scorecard replacing JSON accordions; (5) semantic color, overflow-safe tables, TOC, and legible calendar labels. Keep the single-file HTML, the embedded machine payload, the family-consensus framework, and the honest-uncertainty posture.
